Hello,

This is very interesting... I've never encountered this before, where checkM says it's working with `--check_dependencies` and then fails when running the program. 

A couple of things to try. First, check the log file (in `logger/log.log`). In that file will be the exact command that dRep tried to run with checkM. Try and run that command on your own

Second, next time you run the program, run it with the `-d` parameter. This will produce the actual output that checkM gave when failing, which will be useful for troubleshooting.

Finally, what does the help show if you just run `checkM -h`?

Best,
-Matt

The advantages are that you don't have to rewrite code. It's a wrapper which saves you from writing hefty amount of training-time image augmentation code. Something that doing in scikit-image and PIL will surely make you whine.

A proper method like `invert_augment_keypoints()` does not exist yet. The only way to roughly achieve this would be to place a dense grid of keypoints on the initial image (i.e. one on every pixel) and then augment the keypoints to find corresponding pixels between unaugmented and augmented images. This is obviously going to be slow and not every pixel in the augmented image will have a corresponding pixel in the unaugmented image. A slightly faster method would be to only place keypoints on e.g. every 4th pixel and then interpolate the remaining ones.

Digging further, it appears that there is inconsistent behavior between operating systems. Take the code
```(python)
import pint
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
ureg.meter
```
On Linux this returns `<Quantity(1, 'meter')>`, but on Windows this returns `<Unit('meter')>`. Unfortunately, since on the Linux version the `UnitsContainter` math operations don't appear to be working it does not appear that there is a way to write cross-platform unit comparisons.

It's great that other's are taking some active interest in this PR!  The more voices, the better chance we have on getting it right!

I do agree `precision` might not be the right name of the argument to identify the use.  As pointed out, this could (and likely would) be confused with a string formatting which has a completely different meaning in the language with it's own syntax.  Even though that's probably what most people would use `to_compact` for ... it's actually something very different and should reflect that.

I'll throw another one in: `scale`.  This would be mirroring the [SQL Server definition](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2377174/how-do-i-interpret-precision-and-scale-of-a-number-in-a-database) and [Microsoft Transact-SQL](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/t-sql/data-types/decimal-and-numeric-transact-sql) where:
`scale`:  The number of decimal digits that will be stored to the right of the decimal point.

for our purposes 

`scale`:  The number of decimal digits that will be ~~stored~~ preferred to the right of the decimal point.

Let's brainstorm some more!

### to_compact method argument name suggestions
~~`precision`~~ [@JesterEE]
`threshold` (Would require a different input and processing (int => float)) [@onealj]
`scale` [@JesterEE]

@emmanelletrong
Great work!  I think consistency is important on PRs and changing default behavior is only acceptable when necessary.  While that's just my 2 cents, I can understand why you'd want `X=1` rather than `X=0`.  If `to_compact()` was not already a "thing", I'd say implement it as you'd like it ... but, it is a "thing".  Changing `X=1` may break people's established code.  Would it be minor ... sure, but for what reason?

@onealj
Some good points about non-base10 units.  This can be seen when using `to_compact` with time quantities.  It isn't smart enough to use the registry to find "compatible" units to convert to and just sticks to SI prefixes.
```python
import pint
u = pint.UnitRegistry()
'{:0.2f}'.format(u('1000 min').to_compact())
>>> 1.00 kilominute
'{:0.2f}'.format(u('1000 min').to('sec').to_compact())
>>> 60.00 kilosec
```
However, I would caution automating this too much because in the current state of `pint` the `compatible_units()` method returns a frozenset.  This is a great use of this container ... except for instances like this.  To make sure the output is tailored successfully, there would need to a 'priority' assigned to the possible compatible conversions so that higher priority units were returned when the "criteria" (whatever that is to be) is met for that unit.  This again is very user specific but maybe beyond the scope of what `to_compact` should be trying to figure out.  Here's a hypothetical example where this could cause an undesired output and a hypothetical implementation to address it (which I personally do not like).  Any implementation that does something like this would have to be very clever ... even more clever than pint itself can be currently (shown below).

```python
import pint
u = pint.UnitRegistry()
time = u('6000 sec')
time.compatible_units()  # Note some time scaled units (e.g. minute) are not defined here!
>>> frozenset({<Unit('shake')>,
>>>            <Unit('sidereal_second')>,
>>>            <Unit('second')>,
>>>            <Unit('sidereal_minute')>,
>>>            <Unit('sidereal_hour')>,
>>>            <Unit('sidereal_day')>,
>>>            <Unit('week')>,
>>>            <Unit('work_month')>,
>>>            <Unit('sidereal_month')>,
>>>            <Unit('tropical_month')>,
>>>            <Unit('synodic_month')>,
>>>            <Unit('work_year')>,
>>>            <Unit('gregorian_year')>,
>>>            <Unit('julian_year')>,
>>>            <Unit('sidereal_year')>,
>>>            <Unit('leap_year')>,
>>>            <Unit('eon')>})
'{:0.2f}.format(time.to_compact())  # Lets assume to_compact arbitrarily tries all the compatible units till it finds a "good" one, though this would be a bad approach
>>> 1.67 sidereal_hour
'{:0.2f}.format(time.to_compact())  # Maybe a different one this time ... let's see
>>> 100.27 sidereal_minute
'{:0.2f}.format(time.to_compact(unit_priority=['second', 'minute', 'hour', 'day', 'week', 'month', 'year']))  # 'If' to_compact would do this ... provide yet another argument
>>> 100 minute
'{:0.2f}.format(time.to_compact((unit_priority=['year', 'month', 'week', 'day', 'hour', 'minute', 'second']))  # A different priority
>>> 1.67 hour
```

Also, I don't think specifying the desired unit in `to_compact()` is the right approach.  Using your example above, a user could (and it is better practice) use the `to` method of a pint.quantity to get the same result:
```python
import pint
u = pint.UnitRegistry()
(1.23e3*u.m).to('km')
>>> 1.23 kilometer
```

I think that `to_compact` is solving something completely different.  That is, giving the user the "best looking" representation of a quantity, rather than a quantity tailored to a specific unit scaling.  Since "best looking" is subjective, the function should be configurable to that trait.  Though, the nice thing about 'to_compact' is it's smart for you.  The more you have to give it, (desired units, priorities, thresholds, etc.) the more you can just do it on your own with "manual" conversions to units and scales.

I'd rather see an under-engineered solution that doesn't do it all, but is easy to use rather than an over-engineered solution that takes lots of input to get right.  But, if you want to implement all that, it may prove to be useful and I will be nothing short of encouraging along the way.  Though it's probably way more involved than @emmanelletrong had in mind for this PR.

I do not think this is possible with IPython and Pint as IPython widget does not define any protocol that Pint can implement. But you could implement your own IPython Widget that is Pint aware subclassing the SliderWidget

Indeed units are normalized to the main name as given by the registry as soon as the Quantity is created. This is good because it fails early if a unit is not defined or ambiguous, it speeds up conversions and allows to better cache results. 

Having said that, it would be nice to have a way to keep what the used have chosen. It is easy for single units. But doing it for compound units might be trickier. If you can provide a good API and internal logic, I will be have to move forward with this.

Is ``range(0, 10)[0]`` a ``range``? The behavior upon calling a ``Model`` is guaranteed, but ```model[A](k=1, t=tdata)``` does not call a model. It returns a symbolic component, which is then called with the provided arguments.

For a normal model, we have ``y_i = f_i(x, ...)`` for every component. For a system of ODEs we have ``d(y_i)/dx = f_i(y_1, ..., y_m, x, ...)``. Indexing any ``Model`` returns the symbolic expression for that component and therefore for an ``ODEModel`` it should return ``f_i(y_1, ..., y_m, x, ...)`` (a symbolic expression).

That is why in general ```model[A](k=1, t=tdata)``` simply can't work. For given values of ``y_1, ..., y_m, x, ...`` the symbolic expression ``model[A]`` can be evaluated. But that is off course not what we want here, you want the numerical value of ``y_i``. Therefore the model has to be called through the normal API so that the system of ODEs can be integrated.

Bottom line: indexing models returns the symbolic expression for that component, not a numerical expression. This is fundamental to the whole structure of ``symfit``. They should not be called directly if you need numerical results.

So I really think this should be rewritten to the recommended syntax. I get that this might cause some difficulties, but the order of the return of the call is a ```namedtuple```. So I'm pretty sure this can be abused quite easily by doing 

```python
ans = model(k=1, t=tdata)
print(getattr(ans, A.name))
```

Furthermore, the order of this ``namedtuple`` is alphabetic so that might help.

No worries, and I see what you mean now -- I was only thinking of the "test" meta_features previously, which is why I maybe got confused. But sure, having options for both would be fine :).

So regarding the "test" meta_feature, i.e., the easier case, it would be fine to do the following as you suggested:

```python
def _predict_meta_features(self, X):
    return np.column_stack([regr.predict(X) for regr in self.regr_])
```

However, we should make this a "public" method; that is, renaming it to `def predict_meta_features(self, X)`.

Now, thinking about it a bit, I think it would be good to have 2 methods: the one above and a second one for the training meta features so that people can compute the train meta_features (e.g., later) without calling fit again. Also, per scikit-learn convention, it's maybe confusing if `fit` returns data. 

My preferred solution would look like this though: `predict_meta_features(self, X=None)`. And if `X=None` then the training meta features are returned, and otherwise, the meta features for X are returned. 

I am not 100% positive on that, but the CV as it is currently implemented -- regarding the shuffle argument -- should be deterministic and always return the same shuffling order. (Otherwise we might have to set the random seed somehow manually, because I think there's no random seed parameter in the Kfold class.

So, to summarize what I was thinking of:

Implementing `predict_meta_features(self, X=None)`, which returns the training meta-features if `X=None` and the "test" features if `X=X`. This could be done as a generator, so that the current `fit`method can be refactored to use `predict_meta_features(self, X=X)`

If everything is built under one top-level CMakeLists.txt, the build instructions become simpler since the procedure is always the same. The user just has to change options via cmake-gui/ccmake. For example, `DLIB_BUILD_EXAMPLES` (which should be defined in the CMakeLists.txt with a helpful doc string). 

Same for building shared vs static libs. It's fine to default to build static libraries by default, but the build should use the standard CMake variable for choosing how the library gets built: `BUILD_SHARED_LIBS`.  Defining variables in your CMakeLists.txt like `option(BUILD_SHARED_LIBS "Build dlib as a shared library" OFF)` accomplishes two things:

- Sets the default value you'd like to use for this CMake built-in variable
- Makes the variable show up in ccmake/cmake-gui

I wasn't quite following your timing test so I made the simplest example I could think of for the two ways of formatting rows. When I run it, the large format string with a single call is faster under all the face counts I tried:

```
import timeit
import numpy as np
    
def format_loop(array):
    '''
    Loop through each row and run a format operation per- row
    '''
    result = '\n'.join('{} {} {}'.format(*row) for row in array)
    return result
    
def format_once(array):
    '''
    Create a giant string and run a single format operation
    '''
    result = '{} {} {}\n' * len(array)
    result = result.format(*array.reshape(-1))[:-1]
    return result
    
if __name__ == '__main__':
    # check to make sure functions are doing the same thing
    data = np.arange(1000*3).reshape((-1,3))
    a = format_loop(data)
    b = format_once(data)
    assert a == b
    
    # the order of magnitude is that a large/midsize mesh has roughly 1 million faces
    counts = np.linspace(0.0, 1e6/3, 10)[1:].astype(int) * 3

    # how many runs per function
    test_runs = 10
    result = [['counts', 'loop', 'once']]
    
    for count in counts:
        t_loop = min(timeit.repeat(stmt='f(d)', 
                          setup='d=np.arange(count).reshape((-1,3))', 
                          globals={'count' : count,
                                 'f' : format_loop,
                                 'np' :np},
                          number=test_runs,
                          repeat=3)) / test_runs
                          
        t_once = min(timeit.repeat(stmt='f(d)', 
                                  setup='d=np.arange(count).reshape((-1,3))', 
                                  globals={'count' : count,
                                         'f' : format_once,
                                         'np' :np},
                                  number=test_runs,
                                  repeat=3)) / test_runs
        result.append([count, t_loop, t_once])
        print(result[-1])
```

and I totally agree that the prov bundles are a mechanism to talk about the provenance of the bundle. But I've a little problem with one specific case:  

The following example is a sligtly altered version of the [Bundle1](https://provenance.ecs.soton.ac.uk/store/documents/114710) test case 

In fact I removed for one bundle the `entity(bob:bundle1, [prov:type='prov:Bundle'])` entity 
````
document
  prefix bob <http://example.org/bob/>
  prefix alice <http://example.org/alice/>
  prefix ex <http://example.org/example/>
  
  wasAttributedTo(bob:bundle1, ex:Bob)
  wasAttributedTo(alice:bundle2, ex:Alice)
  agent(ex:Bob)
  agent(ex:Alice)
  entity(alice:bundle2, [prov:type='prov:Bundle'])
  bundle alice:bundle2
    prefix alice <http://example.org/alice/>
    prefix ex <http://example.org/example/>
    
    wasDerivedFrom(ex:report2, ex:report1, -, -, -)
    entity(ex:report1)
    entity(ex:report2, [prov:type="report", ex:version=2])
    wasGeneratedBy(ex:report2, -, 2012-05-25T11:00:01+01:00)
  endBundle
  bundle bob:bundle1
    prefix bob <http://example.org/bob/>
    prefix ex <http://example.org/example/>
    
    entity(ex:report1, [prov:type="report", ex:version=1])
    wasGeneratedBy(ex:report1, -, 2012-05-24T10:00:01+01:00)
  endBundle
endDocument
````

In this case the prov lib returns only the `alice:bundle2` as entity (because the input match the output). 
I would say this is an invalid document because I *talk* about a bundle (`  wasAttributedTo(alice:bundle2, ex:Alice)`) that dosen't exists. But the interesting fact is that the [Prov-Validator](https://provenance.ecs.soton.ac.uk/validator/view/documents/graph65490189435852918/validation/report.html) says this document is totally fine and fulfill all constrains. 

I red the book "Provenance an introduction into PROV"(Luc Moreau & Paul Groth) and the paragraph about bundles says: *A bundle (class prove:Bundle) is a named set of provenance descriptions, and is itself an entity allowing provenance of provenance to be expressed* 

So I think we don't modify the output if we return bundles also as entity, the PROV-Standard declares that a bundle is also an entity. Or maybe I understood something wrong ;)? 

PS: My main problem is that I'm currently developing a library to save prov documents into a neo4j database and I save the bundle as one node (based on the identifier) So I can either return the prov bundle as entity or not but if I do it only in some cases (Just like the input) it is also kind of  inconsistency.

@peterxsu
It's not a simple matter of duplicate names that define identical subcells. Cells could have the same name and different contents. It could be dangerous for users to fall into this trap without knowing it.

In your case, if you *know* that cells with the same name are identical, the following code should give you an idea of what you have to do to merge the files:

```python
import gdspy


def create_gdsii(n):
    fname = 'file{}.gds'.format(n)

    cell1 = gdspy.Cell('REFERENCED', True)
    cell1.add(gdspy.Rectangle((0, -0.5), (1, 0.5)))
    cell1.add(gdspy.Label('Cell from {} '.format(fname), (0, 0), 'e'))

    cell2 = gdspy.Cell('COPY{}'.format(n), True)
    cell2.add(gdspy.CellArray(cell1, 1, 1 + n, (0, 2)))

    lib = gdspy.GdsLibrary()
    lib.add([cell1, cell2])
    lib.write_gds(fname)
    return fname


# Create 2 example files
file1 = create_gdsii(1)
file2 = create_gdsii(2)

# Load first file
lib1 = gdspy.GdsLibrary(infile=file1)
# Extract all cells:
for cell in lib1.top_level():
    lib1.extract(cell)

# Load second file
lib2 = gdspy.GdsLibrary(infile=file2)

# Change references based on cell names
# WARNING: this is no guarantee that the cells are identical!
for cell in lib2.cell_dict.values():
    for element in cell.elements:
        if (isinstance(element, gdspy.CellArray) or
                isinstance(element, gdspy.CellReference)):
            name = element.ref_cell.name
            if name in gdspy.current_library.cell_dict:
                element.ref_cell = gdspy.current_library.cell_dict[name]

# Manually extract all cells skipping duplicate names
for name, cell in lib2.cell_dict.items():
    if name not in gdspy.current_library.cell_dict:
        gdspy.current_library.add(cell)

gdspy.LayoutViewer()
```
Note that, if you are using several files, I'd advise against using the global `gdspy.current_library`. Instead, you can work with a `merged_lib` as follows:

```python
import gdspy


def create_gdsii(n):
    fname = 'file{}.gds'.format(n)

    cell1 = gdspy.Cell('REFERENCED', True)
    cell1.add(gdspy.Rectangle((0, -0.5), (1, 0.5)))
    cell1.add(gdspy.Label('Cell from {} '.format(fname), (0, 0), 'e'))

    cell2 = gdspy.Cell('COPY{}'.format(n), True)
    cell2.add(gdspy.CellArray(cell1, 1, 1 + n, (0, 2)))

    lib = gdspy.GdsLibrary()
    lib.add([cell1, cell2])
    lib.write_gds(fname)
    return fname


# Create 2 example files
file1 = create_gdsii(1)
file2 = create_gdsii(2)

# Load first file into merged library
merged_lib = gdspy.GdsLibrary(infile=file1)

# Load second file
lib2 = gdspy.GdsLibrary(infile=file2)

# Change references based on cell names
# WARNING: this is no guarantee that the cells are identical!
for cell in lib2.cell_dict.values():
    for element in cell.elements:
        if (isinstance(element, gdspy.CellArray) or
                isinstance(element, gdspy.CellReference)):
            name = element.ref_cell.name
            if name in merged_lib.cell_dict:
                element.ref_cell = merged_lib.cell_dict[name]

# Manually extract all cells skipping duplicate names
for name, cell in lib2.cell_dict.items():
    if name not in merged_lib.cell_dict:
        merged_lib.add(cell)

gdspy.LayoutViewer(merged_lib)
```

Idea:

## Analyze data
Search single-stroke symbols which are not "prefixes" of other symbols. For example. `o` is a prefix of `\oplus` or `l` is a prefix of `t`. Those symbols should also be "stable" in the sense that their variance in stroke count is low. Let B be the group of those basic symbols. Examples of this group might be `\sum`, `\int`, `(`, `)`, `\infty`, `2`, `3`, `6`, `8`, `9` `0`, `a`, `c`, `e`, ...

## Segment
Let L be the list of strokes which should be partitioned into a list of list of strokes, where each sublist is one symbol.

1. In order to segment a recordings, analyze each stroke as a single-stroke symbol. Try to find if a stoke is a member of B. Those members of B are called $L_B \subseteq L$. Let $L' = L \setminus L_B$.
2. The 2-stroke classifier (belongs to the same symbol or not) can use a feature "symbols in between" to find a segmentation of L'

Hi, unfortunately nobody has implemented support for raw data widths > 1. I don't have the time to implement this myself but would be happy to accept pull requests to implement this.

> Am I missing something?

Yes. In your example linear counting is being used since the raw estimate is less than 5/2*m. Linear counting estimates the cardinality using

    m*log(m/V)

where m=number of registers and V=number of zero registers. You can see this by modifying your example:

```python
regs = hll.registers()
print sum(map(lambda x: 1 if x == 0 else 0, regs))
print "Estimated cardinality: {}".format(hll.cardinality())
```

In my example I get the output:

    True cardinality: 996
    15413
    Estimated cardinality: 1444.08451191

Note that

    2^14*log2(2^14/15413) = 1444.085

The svpcom implementation handles small ranges (mostly) the same:

```python
 #count number or registers equal to 0
V = self.M.count(0)

if V > 0:
    H = self.m * math.log(self.m / float(V))
    return H if H <= get_treshold(self.p) else self._Ep()
else:
    return self._Ep()
````

As you can see, when V > 0 a linear count is performed. If the linear count estimate is less than the  threshold value (which depends on the error rate used in the constructor), then the linear count is returned. When the linear count is returned there is no additional bias correction. For small ranges I suspect HLL uses the linear count far more than the svpcom implementation though I haven't tested this. 

The problem of zero value registers causing a spike in the relative error near 5/2*m is something I've planned to address for awhile now. I'll try and get it fixed soon :) I'm closing this issue since the example code results in a linear count and isn't an example of zero value registers inflating the HyperLogLog estimate. For clarity I've created a [separate issue](https://github.com/ascv/HyperLogLog/issues/17) to track this.

I think it was me who was arguing that this made sense (or at least seemed sensible), here's the relevant bit:

> I imagine that if the estimated key is a perfect fifth above the true key then you get 0.5 because they are highly related (tonally). The circle of fifths is not symmetric: the fifth of C is G, but the fifth of G is D. So if the true key is a C, estimating a G (a fifth) is closely related, but estimating a perfect fifth down (an F) is not as tonally related (F is the 4th for C, not the 5th) and hence not rewarded at all.

> Estimating a G below the C (ie a perfect 4th down) should still get rewarded, but since this is key estimation I imagine this is octave agnostic right? Hence you only have to look for a fifth "up" (in reality its a chroma circle, so there is no up and down)

Basically, the relationships between scales are not symmetric: estimating a tonic of G when it's really C is not the same as estimating a tonic of C when it's really G.

According to the [documentation](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/api/docker_remote_api_v1.17/#attach-to-a-container) of the docker remote API:

```logs – 1/True/true or 0/False/false, return logs. Default false```

I understand this to mean that by setting `logs` to `True` we're asking the docker daemon to send us the logged output of the container so far. Looking at [attach_socket](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/docker/api/container.py#L25) in `docker-py` we see that the default is for `logs` is `False`.

To be able to fix this issue in docker-compose we need some way to make dockerpty use `'logs': 0`. This should ideally be an option to dockerpty.start. 

In addition someone who knows the history of the current default might want to look into whether it is worth changing it to match the defaults in the Docker API and in docker-py. I suspect the answer is no. :)

I know it's been a while since I raised this issue. I have attempted to compile all the concrete feature requirements for a common syntax tree that have been listed by the various parties, and I have tried to attribute respective parties to each feature requirement. If I have omitted a requirement or notation of the party affiliated with that requirement, my apologies; please note the omission and I will update the list.

My hope is that we can look at this list of requirements and see the common threads, or reason if any of them are mutually exclusive and what would be a compromise. I'm also hoping this will rekindle interest.

1.  The ST should allow for easy traversal, e.g., for calculating McCabe complexity values (@sigmavirus24)
1.  The ST should keep a "lossless" representation of the code; it should preserve spacing information, i.e., should round-trip back to identical contents, including spacing (@Psycojoker, @takluyver, @asmeurer, @davidhalter)
1.  The ST should support use as a pure AST, allowing the user to ignore spacing information.
1.  It should be possible to add a new node to the ST and have the ST determine the appropriate indentation for the node. (@asmeurer)
1.  Association of spacing should be appropriate to the context; for example, in the expression

    ```
    1 + 2
    ```

    the spacing between the `1` and the `+`, is associated with the `+`. The spacing between the `+` and the `2` is also associated with the `+`. (@Psycojoker)
1.  The ST should handle a partial syntax tree, e.g., `a =` (@asmeurer)
1.  The ST should support error recovery (@davidhalter)
1.  The ST should support partial file parsing (@davidhalter)
1.  The ST should support caching (@davidhalter)
1.  The ST should easily serialize to [and deserialize from?] JSON. (@Psycojoker)
1.  The ST should have an intuitive API built for human interaction; this is not designed for compilers (@Psycojoker)
1.  The ST should support Python 2 and Python 3 syntax (@Psycojoker)
1.  The ST should support syntax for versions of Python different than the one on which the program using the ST is running (@asmeurer, @takluyver)
1.  The ST should easily accomodate new additions to the language (@gwelymernans)
1.  The ST should be easy to extend [?] (@Psycojoker)
1.  The library should focus on a simple, flexible ST, leaving higher level features to third-party extension libraries. (@sigmavirus24)
1.  The ST should be performant (@Psycojoker)
1.  An implementation of the ST should be available in pure Python (@almarklein)

This patch assumes that the mirror can offer a top-level `/dist` – for no good reason really. EVERY use of the domain name repeatedly (un-DRY) decorates the domain with `https://…/dist/`, and that could be done once, in option processing (i.e. the global would change to `src_base_url`). The order of `-i` and `--mirror` handling  is correct, but that order matters should be added as a comment (`-i --mirror …` makes sense and should work).

Another side-effect of using a full URL prefix: `--mirror file://…`. :tada:

I would bring this up with the `cffi` people. It seems like they're using a hack for adding special functionality, which relied on `pycparser` incorrectly allowing multiple types in declaration specifiers. They replace `'...'` in certain situations with the name `'__dotdotdot__'`, which they've typedef'd somewhere else.

If you try to compile

    typedef int __dotdotdot__;
    typedef int __dotdotdot__ time_t;

with gcc or clang, both will fail to parse, having interpreted the second `__dotdotdot__` as a declarator.

Maybe we could add a minor way for users of `pycparser` to do this sort of thing, but I'm not sure how Eli feels about this. I do know that `cffi` uses another hack to work around handling of `__stdcall` using `volatile volatile const` which it would be nice to get rid of.

Here's a basic proposal: Add dummy token type called `USERQUOTE`, which can be any sequence of characters surrounded by backticks (or whatever invalid C we decide). Then we say `USERQUOTE` is a valid type-qualifier. Now `cffi` or whoever can insert arbitrary notes to themselves wherever type qualifiers are allowed. I just tested this out and it works with 6 lines of code added.

@tardyp To clarify, the issue is that pip cannot uninstall the current version of pywin32 (whether it's the pypiwin32 build or the pywin32 one), because one of our vendored libraries imports it (and hence locks the DLLs, which is what is causing the permission error on deletion).

There's a fix in pip master, but it can't be released at the moment as we have incomplete changes holding up a release.

Currently, your options are:

1. Use the unreleased pip from git.
2. Avoid needing to uninstall the currently installed version of pywin32 (I guess that's the "unpinning" option).
3. Manually (or via a script that doesn't invoke pip) remove the current pywin32.

It's frustrating to all of us that we can't get a release of pip out with this (and other) fixes, but manpower on pip is severely limited and getting the outstanding work completed is proving to be a challenge.

Sorry, I don't quite understand your question. But there is one thing you seem to be doing wrong. If you use `per_from_id()`, then your handler should be a subclass of `UserHandler`. If you really want to use `ChatHandler`, then you should use `per_chat_id()` in front.

`per_*()` functions have to be paired correctly with `*Handler`. They have to be on the same page, so to speak.

```python
bot = telepot.aio.DelegatorBot(TOKEN, [
    pave_event_space()(
        per_from_id(), create_open, UserHandlerSubclass, timeout=10),

    pave_event_space()(
        per_chat_id(), create_open, ChatHandlerSubclass, timeout=10),
])
```

Simply put, `ChatHandler` looks at messages with a certain `chat` `id`. If you want to perform a conversation in a private chat or group chat, you should use a `ChatHandler`.

`UserHandler` looks at messages with a certain `from` `id`. If you want to handle actions originating from one user (including his chat messages, inline query, etc), you should use a `UserHandler`.

Telepot now updated, version 6.7

I have added 3 subclasses to `TelegramError`:

- `UnauthorizedError`
- `BotWasKickedError`
- `BotWasBlockedError`

I did not include all of your list because of this consideration:

- I want to include only those that are absolutely out of the programmer's control. After all, there can be a thousand way a request may go wrong. I don't want telepot to house a forest of exceptions just to mirror those returned by Telegram.

    Admittedly, `UnauthorizedError` is (somewhat) within the programmer's control, but it is the only unambiguous indication of a bad token in all of Bot API. And it is the origin of this whole discussion. So I will give you that one. :smile: 

I may have disappointed you, but here is a very nice thing which I am sure you will love. For example, if you want to isolate these two:

```
("[Error]: Bad Request: Can't parse message text: Can't find end of the entity starting at byte offset 140", 400)
('[Error]: Bad Request: Type of file to send mismatch', 400)
```

You can do that easily by subclassing `TelegramError` with a list of signature regex:

```python
class ParseMessageTextError(telepot.exception.TelegramError):
    DESCRIPTION_PATTERNS = ['parse message text']

class FileTypeMismatchError(telepot.exception.TelegramError):
    DESCRIPTION_PATTERNS = ['file.*type.*mismatch', 'type.*file.*mismatch']
```

The matching is done **case-insensitively**, so you don't have to worry about the case.

This is the same principle I use to define my own `UnauthorizedError`, `BotWasKickedError`, and `BotWasBlockedError`. It is very flexible. You can easily define your own error classes and catch them.

I hope this issue can now be closed. :smile:

`ProxyManager` is a part of `urllib3`, and `urllib3` does not work with the async version. Async version uses `aiohttp`. You have to override `telepot.async.api` in a different way.

```python
import aiohttp
import telepot.async.api

telepot.async.api._pools = {
    'default': aiohttp.ProxyConnector(proxy="http://some.proxy.com")
}

telepot.async.api._onetime_pool_spec = (aiohttp.ProxyConnector, dict(proxy="http://some.proxy.com, force_close=True))
```

@dpkp any thoughts on this? Would love to get this patch in production without going off on a fork.

First of all, this is how to use byteswap():

    >>> import bitstruct
    >>> bitstruct.unpack('u7u6u3', bitstruct.byteswap("2", bytearray([0xfa, 0x01])))
    (0, 63, 2)

The format "2" means that the next two bytes should be swapped, in this case byte 0 and byte 1.

We don't have to discuss bit fields. It was just an example usage. It's important that the bitstruct module can be used on "any" stream of bits. `break` will only exit the immediate loop, not the whole generator function. Example:

```python
>>> def f():
...   i = 0
...   while True:
...     yield i
...     i = i + 1
...     if i > 2:
...       break
...   while True:
...     yield i
...     i = i - 1
...     if i < 0:
...       break
...
>>> list(f())
[0, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 0]
```

Great catch, 

You're right, it's actually that "shi3u" is not a valid geohash, just some random letters.  Geohash approximate distance is simply counting up the number of leading characters that match, so it will happily take invalid geohashes, while Haversine distance actually converts them. 

With that in mind, I've added a default false flag to the geohash distance called check_validity, which will check that the inputs are in fact real geohashes before running. I've also changed the tests and README to use valid geohashes.

If you are using different class names or method of showing/hiding child rows, the `print_rows` option can also be set as a row selector, e.g.

print_rows : ":not(.filtered):not(.tablesorter-childRow)"

This setting would create the following css definition in the print popup window:

```css
tbody tr:not(.filtered):not(.tablesorter-childRow) { display: table-row !important; }

The problem here is the driver is using TCP/IP which pyodbc has nothing to do with.  pyodbc gives the SQL to [the driver / DM](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Drivers-and-Driver-Managers) and the driver takes over - pyodbc doesn't even know if TCP/IP is being used, named pipes, etc.

There is a [Connection.timeout](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Connection#timeout) you can set to force a failure when nothing comes back.  It's exactly what you want but you need to come up with a time that is not too long, but is longer than any query you are going to make.  Since it is per-connection, you can manually increase it when you know you have a long-running query.

There is a *separate* timer for making connections which is a parameter of the [connect function](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Module#connect).

It seems Oracle does follow the ANSI 92 standard of ignoring trailing spaces when comparing strings (https://docs.oracle.com/cd/A97630_01/appdev.920/a96624/b_char.htm#175), but the issue here may be the result of using parameter values.  [This](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/603245/oracle10-and-jdbc-how-to-make-char-ignore-trailing-spaces-at-comparision) StackOverflow answer suggests that the parameterized values are sent with specific typing that force trailing spaces to be included in the comparison.

I was uncertain about that. I was reluctant to introduce a dependency on a particular library, because users of pyacoustid are likely to have already settled on their own tag-reading library (Mutagen and TagLib come to mind). So it's a weird position to be in.

Anyway, looking at the source, the acoustid server seems really to only want the `mbid` parameter. The remaining parameters are only present so the server can look up the MBID itself if you don't have it. So a "light" version of the file API could just accept the MBID to be sent as a parameter rather than trying to do any tag reading itself. Not a perfect solution, of course.

Possibly. When a model is saved, it's saved as a `.dill` file. `dill` is a drop-in replacement for `pickle`, and it seems possible to load `.pkl` files in other languages, at least based on a cursory google search: https://www.google.com/search?q=loading+a+pkl+file+in+java

It's not something we currently natively support, and we don't really have any plans to in the near future. The pipeline itself uses enough Python logic that it's probably kinda difficult. 

What I really want to do is build in interfaces to interact with auto_ml in other languages. We'd probably spin up a python server on localhost on that machine, and send data to and from that server from whatever the non-python language is. 

If you want to take a crack at that, I'd love to see it! 

You can also use auto_ml to find what works, do all the rapid iteration testing and development, and then write the production code in a different language once you've got a pretty good direction figured out. 

Let me know what you end up doing- I'm curious.

ASCII was chosen as the default encoding for the BibTeX parser because [BibTeX only properly supports 7-bit encodings](https://github.com/brechtm/citeproc-py/issues/20#issuecomment-63963714). If you want to interpret the BibTeX database using another encodings, simply specify it when initializing a ´BibTeX´ object in your code. The exception indicates to the user that he should carefully consider which encoding to use when parsing the file. It could be useful to raise a more explanatory exception though (and having some documentation would be good too, I admit).

I had been thinking about this topic and also had a look at the link provided. The ideas I had were not that far away from those implemented there, but I always end up in the same corner:

  - Deciding/Specifiying if a rule is meant for entering `long`, `short` or valid for both 

Imagine a custom indicator is used as an entry rule:

```python

class SMACloseRule(bt.Indicator):
    lines = ('smaclose',)
    params = (('period', 15),)

    def __init__(self):
        sma = btind.SMA(self.data, period=self.p.period)
        self.lines.smaclose = self.data - self.sma
```

This will deliver 3 *ranges* of values, *positive*, *zero*, *negative*. And one could argue that *positive* means to enter `long` and `negative` means to exit the `long`.

But on exiting the `long` a `short` could also be opened. Not usual for *stocks* but common for *futures*. And that's where one of the keys for the definition of the *API* is.

I'd suggest that you do this by subclassing `Col` and returning a rendered template.

For example:

```python
from flask_table import Table, Col
from flask import Flask, render_template_string

app = Flask(__name__)

HTML_STRING = '''
<html>
  <body>
    {{ table }}
  </body>
</html>
'''

SELECT_TEMPLATE_STRING = '''
<select>
{% for value, display in options %}
    <option value="{{ value }}">{{ display }}</option>
{% endfor %}
</select>
'''

class SelectCol(Col):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.options = kwargs.pop('options')
        super(SelectCol, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def td_contents(self, item, attr_list):
        return render_template_string(
            SELECT_TEMPLATE_STRING,
            options=self.options)


SELECT_OPTIONS = [
    ('a', 'AAA'),
    ('b', 'BBB'),
]


class MyTable(Table):
    name = Col('Name')
    select = SelectCol('Select', options=SELECT_OPTIONS)


@app.route('/')
def index():
    items = [
        dict(name='name1'),
        dict(name='name2'),
    ]
    table = MyTable(items)
    return render_template_string(HTML_STRING, table=table)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True)
```

I'm using `render_template_string` rather than separate template files, just because it makes for a simpler example.

Hopefully that helps you get a `<select>` into a `<td>`. If there's more to what you're trying to achieve, perhaps you could copy+paste a self-contained example, if possible.

@ram-peri  In response to your questions:

1. No, each one of the extension points has its own register function listed here:  https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/pyresttest/resttest.py#L713-L718

The checks on the argument types should be performed within register method, since it will be different for each extension type (generator, validator, test, comparator, etc).  To be honest, we're really only validating the parse function provided to instantiate an instance of that extension.  These should raise an Exception if the method signature for the parse function is invalid.

The main register_extension method in resttest.py needs to log exceptions if extension parsing methods are not valid, but still be able to continue in the event that one extension fails to load.

2. No, there's no reason to protect it.

To hide a column you need to use the `set_column` options not a format. See the [`set_column`docs](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/worksheet.html#worksheet-set_column).

Here is a fix based on your example:

```python
import xlsxwriter as xlsw

workbook = xlsw.Workbook('hello.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet.write('A1', 'Hello world')

worksheet.set_column('A:A', None, None, {'hidden': True})

workbook.close()

```

fig, ax = plt.subplots(nrows=1, ncols=2, figsize=(10, 20))

Thanks for the context @lucidfrontier45. Databases are usually fairly good about batching updates themselves, the expensive part is creating a connection. I would try something like this if you are looking to solve that problem:

```python
wx = database_writer() # This would be your connection
seq(f).map(expensive_operation).for_each(lambda x: wx.insert(x)) # db doesn't write each time
wx.commit() # db might have committed batches of inserts, this forces the rest/all too
```

Yes, this is a problem. The `bin/django` we generate assumes/requires that you already have a settings file. This is obviously not the case if you're trying to **generate** the project in the first place.

The good news: your setup looks fine.

The bad news: bin/django really does need a settings file in the current setup.

Can you try something? Add this to your `[django]` part: 

    dotted-settings-path = django.conf.global_settings

If my guess is right, this allows you to run bin/django to generate your project. Afterwards you'll have to remove the line again.

I don't see how *any* arg-parser could make sense of command-lines where a `--thing` can take 0 or 1 arguments. How can you answer the question, "shall I eat the next arg?".

On Debian at least there's the `sensible-editor` symlink to open the users' favourite editor, but if the goal is "no information leak" opening random editors is certainly not going to give you that feature. (e.g. would you leak an emacs backup file?)

Taking `-` as a file that means "read stdin" is fairly common unix idiom. My inclination would be to encourage using this (i.e. make it more discoverable etc).

Note that you can also put a space in front of your command-line to exclude it from `.bash_history` (if you have `HISTCONTROL` with ignorespace or whatever).

The reason why we want to separate the Swarm and the Topology is because we
want to delineate the particles and how they are connected.

The Swarm holds the data while the Topology holds the interactions among
these datapoints (particles).

The latter governs how everything behaves, etc. And then, we have a
SwarmOptimizer that enables this behavior.

On Wed, 4 Jul 2018 at 00:08 Lj Miranda <lester.miranda@obf.ateneo.edu>
wrote:

> We can keep the neighbor parameter as an attribute to the Swarm class. It
> is flexible given the attrs module.
>
> We initialize the number of particles in the SwarmOptimizer beforehand, by
> that stage we can assign which one is a neighbor if which.
>
> ‘’’python
> t = Ring(static=True)
>
> opt = GeneralOptimizer(n_particles=10, dimensions=2, options=options,
> topology=t)
> # By this stage we already have the indexing information for each
> neighbor. Computing gbest or lbest will just require accessing this
> matrix/data structure. We can keep this matrix either as an attribute to
> the Swarm or SwarmOptimizer.
> ‘’’
>
> The idea is that if you have 6 particles, we create a vector containing
> the  “clusters.” Say we have a Ring topology (static with 2 neighbors),
> then we have a vector:
>
> [1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2] : not necessarily in the order.
>
> Computing lbest for each cluster will then be as simple as accessing which
> particle is in group 1 or group 2, etc.
>
> On Tue, 3 Jul 2018 at 23:56 Aaron <notifications@github.com> wrote:
>
>> Another thing: Currently we only have dynamic approaches for the
>> topologies, except the Star topology which just uses all particles as a
>> reference and therefore does not require special treatment. If we want to
>> implement other static topologies, though, we need a way to include the
>> swarm in the topology class so the swarm has fixed neighbours which do
>> *not* have to be computed in every iteration. At the moment this might
>> not be possible because the topology has no way to permanently assign
>> neighbours to particles. I reckon the best step to take would be to
>> initialize the swarm inside the topology classes and not in the
>> SwarmOptimizer class as it is now. Reasons for that being:
>>
>>    -
>>
>>    *"Topical" connection:* The topology and the swarm already have a
>>    strong topical connection. Initializing the swarm at the same time as we
>>    initialize the topology makes a lot of sense (to me at least).
>>    -
>>
>>    *Less classes:* The difference between the static and the dynamic
>>    version of a topology is then just defined by the placement of the
>>    initialization of the neighbourhood relationships (e.g. Delaunay
>>    triangulation, getting the nearest neighbours, etc.) inside the class
>>    either in the constructor or in the compute_gbest() method.
>>    Consequently, we could implement the static and dynamic version in one
>>    class and use a parameter to decide which one to be used.
>>    -
>>
>>    *Gain of computation speed:* If we don't initialize the swarm in the
>>    Topology classes and use a static topology it is necessary to
>>    calculate the neighbours in every iteration, thus we lose a lot of
>>    computation time to calculate the exact same neighbours over and over again.
>>
>> If we use the swarm as a static attribute
>> <https://stackoverflow.com/questions/68645/are-static-class-variables-possible>
>> in the superclass Topology it might even be possible to use multiple
>> topologies at the same time! Although, I don't know if it is possible to
>> initizalize the swarm in the subclasses afterwards. Any thoughts on this
>> idea?

Hi, the main reason is because Swagger (Open API) prefers explicit over implicit. There is no such primitive ('integer' without format) defined in spec.

You can still support this kind of definition by adding primitives to pyswagger, refer to [here](https://github.com/mission-liao/pyswagger/blob/master/docs/md/tutorial/extend_prim.md) for more details.

Here is a pseudo code that might work for the primitive you described:
```python
from pyswagger import SwaggerApp
from pyswagger.primitives import SwaggerPrimitive
from pyswagger.primitives._int import validate_int, create_int

# create a cusomized primitive factory
factory = SwaggerPrimitive()
# type == 'integer', format == ''
# type == 'integer', format == None
factory.register('integer', '', create_int, validate_int)
factory.register('integer', None, create_int, validate_int)

# init SwaggerApp with customized primitive factory
app = SwaggerApp.load(your_url, prim=factory)
```

In these extreme cases I would still recommend adding some rate limiting on your side of the code. The regular progressbar needs to do quite a few sanity checks which can be omitted in many cases. These take time and can easily be skipped for your purposes.

Simply replacing a regular bar with a slightly filtered one can help a lot:

```
import progressbar
from datetime import datetime

N = 10000000

# Regular bar without filtering
bar = progressbar.ProgressBar(max_value=N).start()
begin = datetime.now()
for i in range(N):
    bar.update(i)
bar.finish()

print 'Duration: %s' % (datetime.now() - begin)

# Bar with filtering
bar = progressbar.ProgressBar(max_value=N).start()
begin = datetime.now()
for i in range(N):
    if i % 100000 == 0:  # Only update once every 10000 values
        bar.update(i)
bar.finish()

print 'Duration: %s' % (datetime.now() - begin)
```
Results:
```
100% (10000000 of 10000000) |############################| Elapsed Time: 0:00:06 Time: 0:00:06
Duration: 0:00:06.821132
100% (10000000 of 10000000) |############################| Elapsed Time: 0:00:01 Time: 0:00:01
Duration: 0:00:01.184203
```

### How does django-s3-storage compare with django-storages?

The [django-storages-redux](https://github.com/jschneier/django-storages) fork of django-storages appears to be the most widely used S3 storage backend for Django. It also supports a variety of other storage backends.

django-s3-storage provides similar features but only supports S3. It was originally written to support Python 3 at a time when the future of django-storages was unclear.

The author of django-s3-storage is not aware of significant differences in functionality with django-storages-redux. If you notice some differences, please file an issue!

So...  On Mac OSX, the standard terminals use a terminfo database that says the backspace key is mapped to ASCII BS = Ctrl-H = 0x08, but they actually send ASCII DEL = Ctrl-? = 0x7F.  What genius thought this was a good idea?!  Sigh.

Looking around the web now that I know exactly what I'm looking for, I find plenty of examples of how this has affected many other projects.  A particularly good write-up is here: https://github.com/sorin-ionescu/prezto/issues/61.  In particular, I think the proper fix is as follows:

    infocmp "$TERM" | sed -Ee 's/(kbs)=[^,]*/\1=\\177/' -e 's/(kdch1)=[^,]*/\1=\\E[3~/' > "$TERM"
    tic "$TERM"
    rm -f "$TERM"

At least this makes the terminals consistent with the terminfo database they're using...  And, as a passing note, it actually makes them match the definitions for the relevant fields on my CentOS 7 box.

Looks like Apple have been made aware of the issue of their terminfo definitions - if I'm to believe http://www.openradar.me/27624224 - but clearly aren't doing anything about it.  Double sigh!

I'll have to think about how best to handle this.  By asserting that I know better than the terminfo database, I'm opening the project to all sorts of other potential issues.

@ReaBx, no, you are mostly right, it is a confusing concept and I get confused sometimes as well. In lifetimes, the definition is:

```python
customers['recency'] = (customers['max'] - customers['min'])
```

In words: 

```
recency represents the age of the customer when they made their most recent purchases. This is equal to the duration between a customer’s first purchase and their latest purchase.
```

In the linked notebook, the author uses the definition:

> recency: time of most recent purchase

That's too ambiguous of a definition. But ultimately the author uses `lifetimes` utils to calculate it, so their summary statistics are the same. 


> I get further confused by the doc's explanation of the recency/frequency graph where they seem to be using the other definition ("Your coldest customers are those that are in the top-right corner: they bought a lot quickly, and we haven’t seen them in weeks.")

Fair - this graphic does cause a lot of confusion. In generating this graphic, we need to set a max time, which in this case is ~40 time periods. Another way to see this is "all customers first bought from me 40 weeks ago". Thus if a customer has a recency of 5, it means they bought at time 0 and 5 _and never since_. Thus they are likely dead (or "cold").

@soynatan I do think it would be nice to be able to have them both in there, as you say.

I am a little concerned that if all or most fields are updated on a given operation that we'd be storing the JSON twice. From a storage perspective, I'm not too happy with that. Using a `null` or `''` could be a solution to this, a marker to tell the consumer that they should look at the full object. Either that, or we `null` or `''` the `object_json_repr` in favor of just showing the delta, in the case where it's all fields that were changed. And by all fields, I mean all fields in the new repr are either not present in the first (if exists), or are different than the old.

If you don't think we should be concerned about double storage, then I don't mind either. And we could make that a settings flag `EASY_AUDIT_NULL_FULL_ON_FULL_DELTA` or something like that.

For the delta, we should probably just stick with showing the mutated (added or changed) fields. 

The alternative is a larger JSON for `{'added': {field_x: value_x, }, 'modified': {field_x: value_x}, 'deleted': {field_x: value_x}}`. It would be a decent interface, but I think it would only end up being a top-level check, so if one of the values itself was a JSONField, you'd get a huge blob with only a small thing inside ti changed.

So, yeah, I vote for the delta just being the `{field_x: value_x}` JSON and the user can piece the chain together as they wish.

For 2) that is the expected behavior. Namely, here is what happens:

* all pages of your API are translated (using a TranslatableModelSerializer means it is translation-aware), prefixed or not.
* you use a queryset with an unspecified language, which hvad understands as *"use whatever language is the default language at the time of the request"*.
* when you use a prefixed version, the language middleware from Django will set the appropriate language as default, so that gets the expected translation.
* when you use the un-prefixed version, Django's language middleware tries to do some magic to guess a language. Hvad will then proceed to use whatever was set, so check the documentation of Django's LanguageMiddleware to see whether its algorithm suits your needs.
* also, by default, if the browser sends a `language_code` field when POSTing, that will override the language setting for this request. That means that, by default, you can POST to the `it` uri and include a `language_code` field giving another language, the field will win over the `it` par in the uri.

All of those behaviors can be overriden. Depending on the specific behaviors you want, you will want to override either `get_queryset` and/or `get_serializer`. For instance you could disable `language_code` overriding and enforce that the correct language is always used. You would do it this way:
```python
from django.utils import translation

    # in your viewset class
    def get_serializer(self, *args, **kwargs):
        # set the serializer to language-enforcing mode
        kwargs['language'] = translation.get_current()
        return super(WebsiteViewSet, self).get_serializer(*args, **kwargs)
```
That would make sense for your prefixed uris. Note that the browser should not send a `language_code` anymore in that case, or a `ValidationError` will be raised.

By the way, about the `language_code` not being output: if you want the `language_code` field to be output in the serialized form, you must include it in the `fields` list.

this is a problem of the current SikuliX version used here, which is 1.1.1.

Since the beginning of Sikuli, the match score was stripped to 2 decimals.

In your case SikuliX will surely evaluate different scores for both images internally (something like 0.999996 and 0.999912). But both are stripped to 0.99.
Another problem of the current implementation is, that every match score > 0.99 is taken as "exact" match.

To tackle those cases in the moment, you have to use SikuliX features, that return the match scores as is. A function doing this is findAll(). But then you have to interpret the result on your own, since it returns an Iterator<Match>. 

Conclusion: for the time being you have to live with this "grey zone"

@stephenrauch  NoSQL databases don't support subselects so all the changes in Mongo code are only interface updates. `query_env` is currently used by SQL dialects to correctly expand subselects which reference tables from parent query. The contents are dialect-specific and you can use it as you see fit. See `SQLAdapter._select_wcols()` for what is passed in it and `SQLDialect.on()` or `SQLDialect.belongs()` for how the contents are currently used. Using `query_env` to [pass data back](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visitor_pattern) from the expression tree would also be a valid use case.

Using e.g. `None` as the default value for `query_env` would require either additional checks whether the parameter can be used or much larger refactoring of adapter code. @gi0baro already proposed a redesign of query string construction so you can find a better solution later.

I'm unable to post on a corresponding JIRA ticket (https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/AIRFLOW-100, https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/AIRFLOW-93) due to lockdown, but I have successfully monkey-patched `ExternalTaskSensor` with a class that looks like this that should be reasonably flexible, what do you think? I tested it locally and it works as expected.

Copied from gitter:
> is anyone else running into issues with the ExternalTaskSensor requiring an exact execution_date ( apache/incubator-airflow#1060 )?
> this kind of functionality works well only when the jobs are being run at well-known times
> but suppose now that one has some tasks in a DAG dependent on those in another DAG (apache/incubator-airflow#6), and maybe he wants to also enable such dependencies to work with triggered runs, whose execution_dates will not fit so nicely into predetermined time intervals
> would it not make sense to perhaps have some sort of +/- tolerance interval on each side of the target date difference to support these kind of "fuzzy queries"?

```python
class TimeRangeExternalTaskSensor(ExternalTaskSensor):
    """
    Monkey patch ExternalTaskSensor to enable fuzzy date searching; this is a hack
    :param execution_date_tolerance_before: how long before the target execution time to search for a task, in seconds
    :type execution_date_tolerance_before: int
    :param execution_date_tolerance_after: how long after the target execution time to search for a task, in seconds
    :type execution_date_tolerance_after: int
    """

    def __init__(
            self,
            execution_date_tolerance_before=0,
            execution_date_tolerance_after=0,
            *args, **kwargs):
        super(TimeRangeExternalTaskSensor, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.execution_date_tolerance_before = execution_date_tolerance_before
        self.execution_date_tolerance_after = execution_date_tolerance_after

    def poke(self, context):
        if self.execution_delta:
            dttm = context['execution_date'] - self.execution_delta
        else:
            dttm = context['execution_date']

        logging.info(
            'Poking for '
            '{self.external_dag_id}.'
            '{self.external_task_id} on '
            '{dttm} ... '.format(**locals()))
        TI = TaskInstance

        execution_date_lower_bound = dttm - datetime.timedelta(seconds=self.execution_date_tolerance_before)
        execution_date_upper_bound = dttm + datetime.timedelta(seconds=self.execution_date_tolerance_after)
        logging.info('Searching for matching task(s) with execution_date between [{}, {}]...'.format(execution_date_lower_bound, execution_date_upper_bound))

        session = settings.Session()
        count = session.query(TI).filter(
            TI.dag_id == self.external_dag_id,
            TI.task_id == self.external_task_id,
            TI.state.in_(self.allowed_states),
            TI.execution_date >= execution_date_lower_bound,
            TI.execution_date <= execution_date_upper_bound
        ).count()
        session.commit()
        session.close()
        return count
```

@ashb I made an assumption that the [logic happening after the example connection creation](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/utils/db.py#L289) was necessary for a database initialization. For the most part, this does not appear to be the case.

I think this brings up the larger question, should the project continue to support both `airflow initdb` and `airflow upgradedb` in their current forms? It appears that `upgradedb` does the work a user expects out of `initdb` and that the extra logic in `initdb` is not necessary to initialization the database. 

ie.

[Creation of example chart](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/utils/db.py#L310)

[Pre-loading of DAG table](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/utils/db.py#L303)


There are two other core pieces of logic inside of `initdb` that I am unsure of. Is the [loading of the known event types](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/utils/db.py#L289) on database initialization necessary? It does not appear to be used beyond a single call at app creation time. If it is necessary the logic should be combined with `upgrade db`

I am also unsure of the [RBAC piece](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/utils/db.py#L328) inside of `initdb`.  If this is necessary we should combine with `upgrade db`.


I am happy to pivot this ticket into consolidation of these two methods, but would like to hear thoughts from maintainers first.

Wez, thanks for the explanation! That's very helpful. 

What I'm doing is based on a request from Stef Penner (I'm also at LinkedIn, working on a team concerned with developer productivity). Our primary Ember application is large, and uses ember-cli and broccoli to build/serve it (this is all in a dev environment). Ultimately when served, everything is under a single root directory with a .watchmanconfig in that directory. Watchman is being used to watch for source changes to cause rebuild+serve. Broccoli ends up creating a ton of 'sane' instances (WatchmanWatcher) for various directories in the tree, and each of those is creating a watchman.client. So we're ending up with hundreds of connections from our process to watchman, ultimately all coming from under the same root dir (at least that's how it appears from my watching the log). I was trying to create a 'WatchmanClient' in the 'sane' project that would wrap a singleton watchman.Client, and have all the WatchmanWatcher instances use that, as opposed to doing a single 'watch-project' and a lot of subscribes (per discussion with Stef). In looking at the WatchmanWatcher code, it never seems to do anything that is outside of the original directory root that was passed to it (not the project root, which for us is the repo root dir, but what I called the watch root). So it appeared that since watch-project returns the "project" root + offset to the requested root dir, I could remove references to the project root entirely from the WW code. That's where I lost putting the 'relative_root' attribute on the subscribe options. 

I'm still unclear - how would I know that 'relative_root' should be used in the subscribe, since it's not in the 'subscribe' description? I had assumed (apparently wrongly) that 'watch-project' would then imply that any subscribes done after that would be from the project root. That's my confusion. It's actually preferable to me that they are from the 'watch root' for each subscribe, since we don't appear to need the project root info at all in responding to the subscription messages.

Sorry this is so long! And again, I do appreciate the help. Suggestions are definitely encouraged!

This solution in https://github.com/plone/plone.api/pull/355 is not really beautiful but seems to cover all the use-cases:

```python
plone_user = user.getUser()
principal_ids = list(plone_user.getGroups())
principal_ids.insert(0, plone_user.getId())
roles = set([])
pas = portal.get_tool('acl_users')

            roles.update(adapter.getRoles(pid))
return list(roles)
```

 - records when sound is detected, until some silence for ~2 secs
 - write to disk as ogg/opus file (this is what firefox produces)
 - python websocket server that waits for the addon to send some sound
 - converts to the same kind of audio file as the ones we trained the network against, WAV/PCM
 - passes over to Tensorflow Serving
 - passes back decoded string to the addon, over websocket
 - addon interprets what server could decode
 - open website in a new tab command working

Next steps:
 - run this with really trained model
 - add more commands:
  - switch to an open tab
  - scroll up/down
  - zoom in/out

I've tried ~6 times in a row and it occurs each time with Firefox Nightly (all addons disabled, Windows 10 x64). Does not reproduce in Chrome.

**Detailed STR:**
1. Close all other Treeherder tabs
1. Visit https://treeherder-prototype.herokuapp.com/#/jobs?repo=mozilla-inbound
1. If logged in, log out
1. Open developer tools, switch to storage tab (enable if needed), delete all cookies and localstorage entries, then leave the developer tools open at the storage tab
1. Refresh the page
1. Click login/register
1. In the new tab that opens, click through the auth0 "select your username" page
1. Wait for the new tab to be closed, returning you to the original tab
1. Confirm header bar shows logged in, and that the still-open storage tab shows cookie set and localstorage showing `treeherder.user` and `treeherder.userSession` as populated
1. Press `ctrl+L`, `ctrl+C`, `ctrl+T`, `ctrl+shift+E`, `ctrl+L`, `ctrl+V`, `enter` (so the URL is opened in a new tab with the developer panel open prior to page load, so it picks up the /user/ request)
1. Check if header bar shows logged in
1. In the network panel (should already be opened and active) select the `/api/user/` response and switch to the "response" tab to confirm the payload
1. Switch back to the storage tab to check cookie and localstorage entries
1. Switch back to the original tab and check the header bar there too
1. Refresh the original tab
1. Check header bar and `/api/user/` response again


**Expected:**
* All tabs remain logged in after the initial login.
* localstorage `treeherder.user` and `treeherder.userSession` populated with non-empty values.

**Actual:**
* After login:
  - the header bar of the initial tab shows as logged in. Localstorage and cookie set as expected.
* In the newly opened second tab:
  - the header bar shows "login/register" both during pageload and after
  - the cookie is set, however `treeherder.userSession` has been removed, and `treeherder.user` has changed to `{"is_staff":false,"username":"","email":"","loggedin":false}`
  - however the response to the `/api/user/` call was as expected (`[{"username":"mozilla-ldap/emorley@SNIP.com","is_superuser":true,"is_staff":true,"email":"emorley@SNIP.com"}]`)
* Upon returning to the 1st tab:
  - the header bar has now changed to "login/register"
* Upon refreshing the 1st tab:
  - the header bar still shows logged out
  - localstorage still cleared out
  - however the `/api/user/` response still shows me logged into the backend

This issue actually also highlights a different pre-existing bug - that when logout is called from the UI, it only clears localstorage and doesn't call logout on the Django backend. This doesn't need to be fixed here (seeing as its pre-existing), but let's open a bug to track :-)

@edmorley , I noticed that https://github.com/mozilla/treeherder/pull/3144#issuecomment-360854344 only happens when the browser fails to persist localStorage (in my case FF private browsing). To know whether login will work or not,

1. Open a browser and navigate to https://treeherder.mozilla.org
2. Open the developer tool and head over to the console
3. Write: `localStorage.setItem('test', 1);`
4. Make sure you can read it with `localStorage.getItem('test');`
5. Open a new tab (CMD+T) and navigate to https://treeherder.mozilla.org
6. Open the developer tool and head over to the console
7. Write: `localStorage.getItem('test');`

If the last step outputs `1`, then login will work. Otherwise, it won't work. The browser is supposed to persist localStorage across different windows of the same domain. So far, I only see this behavior with FF private browsing. Works on Chrome (normal + incognito) and FF.

I have been pondering over this particular implementation for quite some time, but cannot find a good solution of it.

Essentially, when implementing this, we need to call the `cli()` function and then check if we have the correct scraper. Anything else, in my eyes, is not really testing but writing a function that gives you a pre-determined answer at point of writing.

At the moment, `cli()` is a closed sequence, that when called, checks the arguments passed on, chooses a scraper accordingly and then starts downloading a build if possible.

The only way I have found to create a decent test for it, is to break up `cli()`into two parts. The "matching-the-arguments" part and the "downloading" part (Passing arguments in_between the two)

Q1: Can anyone think of a better, more elegant or simpler way?

Q2: Is it actually feasible to break up the `cli()` function, to allow for more effective testing? We don't have to implement test-driven development, but we are going in the direction of having tests in place for each function, anyways...

Looking forward to a constructive discussion :)

But anyway going back to the original question: I'm increasingly convinced the right thing is to merge this PR as it is.

Having multiple tasks with same name isn't great – it screws up the idea of having a task register that can be used for multiple things (a) command line integration (b) dynamic requirements (c) assistant worker. I really think (a), (b), and (c) should be consistent.

It's not like this PR will break that much... the only thing it will break is if you *directly* invoke `TaskX` from the command line and there are more than one `TaskX` available but only one that implements the `run` method. You can still use it internally in Python using requires etc.

The easiest way to fix this in a one-liner for Spotify is to just add `task.namespace('external')` at the top of `external_tasks.py` and reset it at the end of the file. This isn't super beautiful, but does the trick. We can probably make the namespacing functionality better in a later PR

Thanks for the pull request.  I'm hesitant to change this, because I'd prefer to stick to what the [RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc952) says to use for valid hostnames.  There's a good [summary here](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hostname#Restrictions_on_valid_host_names):

> The Internet standards (Requests for Comments) for protocols mandate that component hostname labels may contain only the ASCII letters 'a' through 'z' (in a case-insensitive manner), the digits '0' through '9', and the hyphen ('-'). The original specification of hostnames in RFC 952, mandated that labels could not start with a digit or with a hyphen, and must not end with a hyphen. However, a subsequent specification (RFC 1123) permitted hostname labels to start with digits. No other symbols, punctuation characters, or white space are permitted.

>  other than that is Boto3/botocore's default behavior to keep retrying the API with exponential backoff until AWS returns a 503/RequestLimitExceeded error or succeeds?

The default behavior is to retry until we the request succeeds or until max_retries is set (https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/_retry.json#L61-L67).

I don't have any guidance in general about finding a good balance between lambda timeout times and boto3 retries.  I suspect finding a good balance would be highly dependent on the specific task you're trying to accomplish.

This isn't really a "veto" thing, because you veto something that would otherwise pass, it's more like a "should pass anyways" kind of thing, in other words, it takes a failing transaction and makes it passing.

Conceptually I don't have a problem exactly with the idea of a "should pass anyways" hook, the biggest concern I have is that it may be a footgun. Raising an exception means that it's possible a bunch of code gets skipped before it hits the exception handler and it's hard to tell what that code is going to do. This may mean that ultimately transactions end up failing because some bit of code in a tween didn't get run. Is there a use case that isn't http exceptions? If it's just the http exceptions then what's wrong with (as @bertjwregeer pointed out) just returning the httpexception when you want the view to be "successful" and reserve raising for when the view should be considered "unsuccessful"?

I'll have to look deeper into this, but here's some background on what the error means, and my initial guess.

We store an object (TrackedRequest) in a thread-local variable, and then call `start_span()` and `stop_span()` on it, as we enter and exit instrumented sections of your code. These calls can nest, with several starts before a stop is ever called.

My guess is that the channels code breaks the 1 request per thread assumption we have in our agent, making those start and stop calls not match up. So at some point, a stop is called where no start happened, resulting in the exception you see.

I changed the title of this issue to test the channel support, with daphne and ASGI. Separately, there will be a change toon to prevent mismatches from ever raising. Unexpected agent state shouldn't 
 cause visible exceptions.

I think it might be realistic to optimize this ourselves.

Using `skimage`, we’re doing RGB to XYZ to Lab to LCH and back again. Now, everything in that pipeline is ufuncs, and for example Lab↔LCH is [basically only Cartesian↔polar](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/color/colorconv.py#L1393-L1397). (I say “only”, which is a bit unfair – `hypot` and `arctan2` will be noticeably slower than, say, `+` and `*` on big arrays. But my point is that there isn’t [a colossal pillar](https://twitter.com/pisscop/status/67709754859995136) of mysterious calculus in there or anything.)

My hunch is that `skimage`’s speed hit is mostly from the overheads of nested function calls and reindexes/assignments that hinder transparent optimization. I suspect that the actual math is not that scary, and probably tractable by algebraic simplification down to a couple lines. That’s just a hunch, so check me.

What we lose is the conceptual clarity of the chain of explicit conversions between well-defined colorspaces. I see that as an acceptable though significant cost, but I could be persuaded.

Given the existing behaviour, and the number of other things being worked on at the moment, it almost certainly isn't worth the hassle of updating `pipenv sync` to also imply `pipenv clean`, especially as we can assume that there are already folks out there relying on the current behaviour.

The main user-centric rationale for doing things the way `pipenv` currently does them is that when using source control, the current `pipenv sync` can be undone by reverting to a previous version of the lock file, and then running the sync operation again. By contrast, when `pipenv clean` removes packages from the current virtual environment, that's it - if you want to add them back, you have to read the list of what was uninstalled, and add them back individually.

It's not clear to me who should be responsible for the fix. Should `venv` detect that it's being executed from within a `virtualenv`, or should `virtualenv` create an environment that's compatible with `venv`? On the one hand, `virtualenv` predates `venv`. On the other, `venv` is part of the standard library.

Regardless, here's the workaround that I currently have in tox-venv:

https://github.com/tox-dev/tox-venv/blob/58401663fda66dfba4f344553525c73d57432d5e/src/tox_venv/hooks.py#L10-L49

The executable path detection could be improved, as it currently just looks for `python3`. eg, you may want `python3.6`, but `python3` points to `python3.4`.

Those are pretty old pip and setuptools versions.

Could you also show the output of ```python -c "import sys; print sys.path```? The reason is that I suspect your pip 1.4 was installed as an egg and is getting forced to the front of sys.path (ahead of the wheel bundled in virtualenv) with the easy_install ```pth``` hacks...

@stefanv re benefits of type hinting:

1. Check correctness of functions at write time instead of run time. See this screencast for an example: https://twitter.com/jakevdp/status/935938990313676800. Many IDEs are starting to have type checking built in. See also https://pyre-check.org/. The benefits of type checking for catching errors is well documented.
2. Ensure our type annotations are correct and consistent. Currently they are a hodge-podge of "Array, shape (M, N, ..., P)", "(M, N, ..., P) float array", etc.
3. Allows us to answer questions such as, "what percentage of skimage functions are 2D-only?" 

On the other hand, I have to say that function annotations in Python are pretty ugly. Here's a couple of attempts:

```python
def disk(radius : int, dtype : NpDType) -> BoolImage2D:
```

```python
def watershed(image : ImageND, markers : Union[LabelImageND, int],
              connectivity : int = 1, offset : Optional[Tuple[int]] = None,
              mask : Optional[BoolImage] = None, compactness : float = 0.,
              watershed_line : bool = False) -> LabelImageND:
```

I wrote these trying to show the benefits of self-documentation thanks to types, but to me they just look super cluttered now. So, I'm more in favour of stub files than inline annotations now.
