@jni: I just improved the the PR taking into consideration your feedback. Honestly, its up to you and the BDFL team with regards to how you want to deal with types, I just wanted to add flexibility to the algorithms which I think this PR achieves without imposing my point of view on the codebase.

1. warping now allows you to specify the dtype of the output. This enables higher level algorithms to choose the precision of this "intermediate step".
2. The default is still to upvert everything to float64 and not to preserve the range.
3. The intermediate computation type is determined by the homography matrix. This is a weird hack. I wish I could just let the compiler decide what the appropriate intermediate type is. For example: uint8 computation can probably benefit from potential speedups from single point arithmetic. Though maybe that is "premature optimization". Specifically, this is commit: https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/3253/commits/8262d7379abf6e99e43fbc3c82b1c6d6ad5b9abd I'm kinda impartial to this so if you want, I can take it off.
4. I backed away from complex interpolation. To avoid code duplication, it really needs to be done with C/C++ macros so that the compiler can choose the appropriate types. I don't know how to do in Cython. Apparently you can use Jinja or something, but maybe just writing straight C code is easier. I also don't know how to combine this with point #1 as you cannot have complex->float casting.

Here's a remaining algorithmic difference between the Python and C++. Since the C++ stores a `SegmentOverlap` struct, it doesn't have to recompute these overlaps. This Python code basically computes the overlaps for each matching segment twice (or, rather, the matching segments in bursting columns). The C++ used to do the same thing -- this was the reason I changed it to store segment + overlap pairs.

This is true independent of your change, and it doesn't affect the results, so I'd be fine with us leaving it this way and deciding later whether to keep optimizing.

I think maybe you did a `git push --force` and caused your comment on your old commit to get deleted. Copy-pasting from email:

> ..this is a bit confusing; tm.compute() returns void and neither takes any output-vector as a param, so I'm guessing the "result" is in the state of the TM (wasn't the Py-TM implemented as functionaly, i.e state-less?), so the result must be accessed by some tm.getXXX()?
>
> And then, TP has param outputMode, which lets you set what cells propagate to the output (active, predicted, both(default)). Am I supposed to do that manually with TM? Would be nice to unify the behavior.

A couple points:

- A unified API surface between TM and the TP isn't really a priority. That's partly what the network API is for. The experience of using the TM and TP have been unified already, via the TPRegion. Of course, that's currently written in Python. So the eventual solution is to add C++ regions for the TM and TP. (Maybe a single region for both? I haven't really thought about it.)
- I think the TM is pretty intuitive. It's a data structure. The compute method is basically a `push` or `feed`. The word "output" doesn't mean anything in the context of TM. It has active cells and predictive cells. If people find reason to use its active and predictive cells in some machine learning algorithm, cool, they're free to do that. The TM doesn't know why it's being used, just as a queue or hashmap don't know why they're being used.

Nope I haven't, but I doubt it would work in this case. If I remember correctly, the problem was due to Django persisting the session data to the db at the END of the request. The long running request, which is started early, will overwrite the session data from the later requests, even if the later requests indicate activity.

In #38, the user doesn't move the mouse because the main interaction with the page is listening to the audio. Therefore, no activity indication was sent to the server.

In this case, the user actually moves the mouse during the long running requests and sends off activity data to the server. However, when the long running request returns, it overwrites all the previous session data, leading the next request to trigger the logout response.

Unfortunately, I'm not sure there can be a fix for this, as it's an inherent behavior of how Django handles the session data. If there's a way to flush the session data periodically during the long running request, that should fix the problem.

cc @inveniosoftware/triagers

### Purpose
Thanks for the comments so far. Let me just reiterate why we want to change the license:

- Library: Invenio has been transformed from a software application into a library that you can mix/match as you see fit. Most libraries in the world are released under a permissive license (LGPL, Apache, BSD, MIT, ...) to not impose a license on downstream applications. Take e.g. Invenio where we only had one GPL dependency out of our ~200 dependencies. Having a permissive license for Invenio makes it easier to combine with other applications and makes it easier to adopt. Also, having a permissive license, doesn't prevent further downstream product to be GPL (e.g. we don't currently plan to change license of Zenodo, CDS etc from GPL).
- Commercialization: We want to allow companies and institutions to keep their add-ons private if they so desire (and forks for that sake). More than halfs of CERNs funding (tax payer money) is going back via e.g. procurement to member states to generate economic benefits and competencies in companies in the member states. This is an explicit desire from our member states, that money invested in research at CERN goes back out into society. Having a permissive license ensures more possibilities for commercial exploitation.

### Source code contributions
Note that I specifically mention in the proposal that the **the purpose of the license change is NOT aimed at increasing source code contributions** to Invenio. 

We can debate, whether or not increased source code contributions would be a derived effect of a license change, however again, that's not why we want to change the license. Increased source code contributions as a lot more to do with an open community where people see the benefit of contributing than the license. Also, note that we already have an existing project on [making contribution easier](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/opensource/blob/master/projects/making-contributing-easy.md). But again, this is not the purpose of the license change.

### Impact outside CERN?

I'd like to hear more from non-CERN users and contributors how a license change would impact them. E.g. would a license change from MIT to GPL e.g. prevent/allow you from contributing more?

> Fixes references to `CFG_PREFIX`, which in reality referred to the location of installation of invenio executables. These should be in the `PATH` environment variable.

IMHO the `CFG_PREFIX` is fine as it's defined in `invenio/base/config.py`.

It is preferred to list individual years, because this technique allows for "year holes" and for different copyright holders.  (Since, generally speaking, it is not true that every file is touched every single year during  the YYY1-YYY2 interval.)  

Quoting from [GNU GPL Howto](http://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-howto.html):

"""The copyright notice should include the year in which you finished preparing the release (so if you finished it in 1998 but didn't post it until 1999, use 1998). You should add the proper year for each release; for example, “Copyright 1998, 1999 Terry Jones” if some versions were finished in 1998 and some were finished in 1999. If several people helped write the code, use all their names.

For software with several releases over multiple years, it's okay to use a range (“2008-2010”) instead of listing individual years (“2008, 2009, 2010”) if and only if every year in the range, inclusive, really is a “copyrightable” year that would be listed individually; and you make an explicit statement in your documentation about this usage."""

The ELF class isn't wrong, it's because they target 32 bit ARM, hence the problem when hostpython tries to import them on the desktop. This happens because the hostpython is really an x86 python binary in an arm python-install, and the workaround here is to use the hostpython from its own (x86) install dir but target the arm one. This is normal and should (barring other issue) run fine on an actual android device.

I guess not only is this information preserved in `classes_`, but it can also be deduced by rerunning `LabelBinarizer.transform()` on the set of original labels. This is important because without this guarantee that `LabelBinarizer` is fully deterministic, it would be impossible to write custom scoring functions that require probabilities. For example, the built-in `log_loss` metric starts by calling `LabelBinarizer.transform()` without actually being able to see the `classes_` attribute and without the access to the `LabelBinarizer` instance from the estimator.

The next steps here are to check for BinOps in the ast of the input. This will happen here: 

https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/72604f40314008e562ba47936dcc183b51166b72/fire/parser.py#L97-L98

If there are any BinOps in the ast (this requires traversing the ast to determine), then we will raise a ValueError as above, rather than parsing this non-literal. The only exception to this is that the BinOps + and - used in the context of expressing a complex number (e.g. 2 + 3j) are allowed.

The [Python 2.7.3 implementation of literal_eval](https://github.com/enthought/Python-2.7.3/blob/69fe0ffd2d85b4002cacae1f28ef2eb0f25e16ae/Lib/ast.py#L67) demonstrates how we can do this AST walk to determine if any non-allowed BinOps are present.

I've made some more updates to this branch, but I think I'm done now.  In particular, the showcase for many of the features here is in [PR into the galxim_extra repo](https://github.com/esheldon/galsim_extra/pull/4) in case you want to check that out.

Notable additional features and improvements:
- Allow a template string to look like `:field` which uses another field within the current configuration dict as the template.  (It had been permissible to write `file_name:field` to take from a different file.)
- Made the output.nfiles information an override-able method `getNFiles` so custom output types can determine this programmatically if they want.
- Fixed a bug in Transformation.shoot if the wcs has a reflection.  (The flux was coming out negative in this case due to a missing `abs`.)
- Added `skip` as an item in the stamp field, rather than just in gal or psf.  It works the same way, but for some reasons to skip, it makes more sense to put it there, so I wanted to allow that.
- Allow custom modules to add their own valid `index_key`s by making `valid_index_keys` a public list, rather than hard-coded within the function that uses it.
- Made the way the extra outputs get handled OutputBuilder methods, so they can be overridden by custom modules if it makes sense to do so.
- Various minor bug fixes that turned up along the way.

I also made some changes that sped up a few things that were taking a long time for my FocalPlane run.
- Ported the CD, PV and AB calculations in GSFitsWCS into C++, since the numpy code was rather slow and used a huge amount of memory to compute a sky image.
- Sort of "compile" aspects of the config processing to not have to parse the same strings over and over again running through all the objects.  e.g. Eval statements now eval a `lambda` expression, which then gets called with the new variables each time.  This is a lot faster than eval-ing again for each object.  Similarly, Current types store the location in the dict where it should evaluate rather than parse the key name each time.

Hey guys, sorry for the long non-response on this one... I have been playing a little in spare moments on vacation, quick update.

## Pupil plane image results
Wow, I had no idea this would use so much RAM in high accuracy mode!  Basically way more than my laptop has, and it never completed in reasonable time (e.g. overnight, even.)  Sorry to not be much use here!

## Approximate struts results
I could get the `approximate_struts` image to be better quality and sub 1.e-4 in terms of higher frequency (i.e. ultra-bandpass) gunk without unreasonable memory costs, simply by upping the `oversampling` to 3.5 from 3.0 when building the `OpticalPSF` within `wfirst_psfs.py` (around line 138):
![fig2_6_after](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454961/8273093/505bac0e-1813-11e5-8f8a-b38612153ab3.png)

The `pad_factor` keyword here has little effect on the amplitude of the bumps, so so much for that idea.  So in terms of this PR I think that I'm happy as is, but might suggest bumping the 3.0 up to 3.5 for `oversampling`.

What I did notice and which I found interesting was that increasing the oversampling helps smooth those bumps, but it does not impact the "continuum" i.e. "green cross" pattern stuff.  Here is an example with oversampling set to 5:
![fig2_6_after_os5](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454961/8273171/d93a604a-1815-11e5-8e01-d2002c6a8dab.png)

Note there is also a slight tail, at the edge of the sharp drop in the MTF at the bandlimit, that is also not reduced by increasing the oversampling.  I don't know the source of this artifact for certain, but my hypothesis is that it is caused by the representation of the curved edges of the pupil using a grid with straight, and sharp, vertical and horizontal boundaries. The reason (I'm guessing) this doesn't "go away" if you increase oversampling or the `pad_factor` is that you aren't in fact reducing the amount of vertical or horizontal edge in the image, you are just switching from vertical to horizontal more often.

I don't know if this is right, and I certainly shouldn't hold up this PR more while I try out the `float` pupil plane idea to try and see if this reduces the effect.  (I'm betting it will.)  I might make an issue for this if there is interest.

Moreover, this idea doesn't really help with the pupil plane image version of the WFIRST PSF.  Does anyone know if there is a vector-graphical representation of the same pupil plane image, in some decipherable polygonal format? (I'm guessing the raster image used here must have come ultimately from some sort of CAD model...)  Because if so it would be really good to try and use that somehow - either by making a smoothed boundary version of what we already have, or using it directly.  But that's an even bigger project.  Probably best to work out if what we have in this PR is good enough.  My preference for sub 1.e-4 artifacts is definitely conservative.

About SEDs:

> Actually, I think I'd recommend getting rid of the sed keyword in __init__ and the withSED method 
> entirely. The SED of a chromatic profile is really only completely unambiguous for a separable profile 
> (for which it doesn't make any sense to interpolate). For an inseparable profile, the SED by definition 
> varies with position. One could argue I suppose that the SED in this case could refer to the spatially 
> integrated flux at each wavelength, but I can't think of a situation where I would actually want to 
> create different chromatic profiles this way. Let me know if I'm just not being sufficiently creative 
> though.

So let's ignore ```ChromaticOpticalPSF``` for the moment and think about the case where someone has defined an ```InterpolatedChromaticObject``` that they want to correspond to a galaxy light profile.  (Thinking about concretely how/why they might do this: let's consider for example the use of HST data in multiple bands to represent non-parametric galaxy light profiles.  I could imagine that this might involve some ```InterpolatedImage``` representation of the light profile as a function of wavelength, and since those are slow to create and render directly, the ```InterpolatedChromaticObject``` functionality could come in handy.)

In this case, what I mean by ```SED``` is the relative weight to give the images they infer for different values of wavelength.  In other words if you ignore the PSF, then the light profile is essentially the integral of bandpass(w)*SED(w)*image(w).  I guess the ambiguity here is that the stored images for each wavelength might have some non-unity flux.  If they were always constrained to have unit flux, then image(w) for any arbitrary wavelength w would have unity flux given our linear interpolation in wavelength.  In that case, I think that the concept of an SED as a weight function makes sense, and i think it corresponds to exactly what you said - "the SED in this case could refer to the spatially integrated flux at each wavelength".  Do you agree with that statement and that it might be useful in the context I described, or am I off base here?  I do agree with you that it's not the same as an SED in the traditional sense, since the actual SED at each position will be different.

In the case that the object is convolved with an acrhomatic PSF, the light profile should be that PSF convolved with \int bandpass(w)*SED(w)*image(w).  If the PSF is chromatic then I guess it has to be the integral of bandpass(w)*SED(w)*[PSF(w) x image(w)] (where x = convolution) but here we lose the speedup due to interpolation since we have to instantiate an ```InterpolatedImage``` at each wavelength value.

Hi @robmadole, After integrating some logs in a local copy of the ES source I finally tracked the issue down and I believe it is a legitimate one. What I found is when the `addPack` hook runs, it automatically calls upon the `autoReplace` function which attempts to inject the SVG into the DOM. The injection functionality is working but what happens is this. 1. The core `fontawesome` library is completely loaded when the page load which means all `` elements are available for it to find and use 2. I add an icon to the library which runs `autoReplace` again 3. I add another icon, but because the first addition runs `autoReplace`, the DOM element is no longer available for the library to use I believe this is a basic oversight and that DOM elements without a matching library definition shouldn't be manipulated in any way. Hope that helps explains the issue in more detail.

Thanks for reporting the issue. Indeed, a `menage` has at most one `conjoint` and one `personne_de_reference`.

Your feedback will be obviously taken into account, and we'd be curious to know more about how this issue impacted your use of OpenFisca.

- How long did it take you to figure out the proper way to declare the menage members ? 
- Is this a problem that you encountered once and solved, or does it come regularly ? 
- Any other detailed feedback will of course be welcome 🙂 .

There are a couple of things going on:
1. The display format shouldn't change whether a log entry is filtered out or not, but it does. If journalctl is going to omit displaying a log entry because it has no MESSAGE field, then it should be consistent across all formats. The smart way, IMO, is to always display the entry, rather than to never display the entry. I feel that that's the smart way, because even with the terse formats, a fair amount of information is still displayed even when there is no MESSAGE field set. With the more verbose formats, a ton of stuff is displayed.
2. The journal has a notion of catalog, which you know about. With the catalog it actually does start to make sense to have an entry without a MESSAGE field and the expectation that the information would be filled in by means of the catalog. In fact, a MESSAGE-less entry would be in the spirit of journal's new structured format, trending away from the older approaches to the journal of only logging free form messages. Require a MESSAGE entry always and you'll get the same kind of goofiness that you get with emails, where people put "SSIA (Subject Says it All)" in the body of the email just to get it past their mailer, and so forth.

This discussion really highlight the ambiguity of terminology used in the field. Personally, I agree with Christian for reconstruction=database of known existing reactions within an organism/cell type and model=reconstruction that is constrained to represent the case study it is used for. In this context, I also agree with Daniel for the fact that the reconstruction should not be pre-constrained but includes the necessary information to constrain it. For example, when a genome-scale reconstruction is published, this library is most often validated using different experimental data. To this end, I think the idea of providing the necessary scripts to reproduce these condition with an automatic constraint generation would be really valuable. 

Moreover, I'd like to underline that terminology like condition - or context- specific model are also broadly used to define models that have been "extracted" from a genome-scale reconstruction using experimental data but only include the part of it that is "active" under the defined condition/ context, doing so are no more a genome-scale model (i.e. a genome scale reconstruction constrained to observed environmental conditions). This aspect is not mentioned in the manuscript for the moment but, in these cases, memote could be a really useful tool to check the validity of these kind of extracted models. Indeed, when you use metabolomic and/or transcriptomic data to extract a context-specific model nothing ensure that this latter is still biologically meaningful. Using the comparative assessment of models presented in memote could allow users to ensure that the extracted model behave globally as the original one (no violation of universal constraints, etc).

> Why is this gone?

I have reexamined this bit of code. In all cases, the keys are the native `str` type. Not always unicode or always bytes. So I have updated to take this into account.

It was originally removed because, on Python3, it compared a `bytes` object to a `str` object with the `in` operator. Comparing these two types is usually a code smell as the code should _expect_ one or the other. When the `-b` flag is passed to `python`, it results in the warning "BytesWarning: Comparison between bytes and string", the change fixes this.

So yeah, while it does look like authenticating user credentials in basic auth is not technically in the spec, who knows what servers are doing in the wild for people. This version fixes the issue without changing the behavior too much for people who weren't passing `client_id` in. There is a chance this could cause breakage for some people who used this implementation as a reference for what to do on the server side instead of going to the spec but I also think it fixes an issue where the client was not following the spec for the password grant type so it's good to do.

AFAICT, the [docs for the `jobReference` element of a job resource](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/rest/v2/jobs#jobReference) say that `jobId` is a required field.  The ["managing jobs" docs](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/managing_jobs_datasets_projects#generate-jobid) state that supplying a job ID is best practice:

>  If you call jobs.insert without specifying a job ID, BigQuery will create a job ID for you, but you will not be able to check the status of that job until the call returns. Moreover, it may be difficult to tell whether the job was successfully inserted or not. If you use your own job ID, you can check the status of the job at any time and you can retry on the same job ID to ensure that the job starts exactly one time.

@cplee This makes sense now! Serverless Application Repository does not allow you to provide a value of `*` for the resource name (in your case, it is the name of CodePipeline resource). 

This might sound a bit counter-intuitive, but it is done to make apps more secure. As a publisher, the best practice is to parameterize the resource name using Template Parameters, and let the consumer decide the pipeline to give access to, at the time of deployment. Consumers could even choose a value of `*`. 

```
Parameters:
  PipelineNameParam:
     Type: String

Resources:
   ...
        CodePipelineReadOnlyPolicy: !Ref PipelineNameParam
```

This way, your app is generic when you publish, and consumers can give access to necessary resources when deploying. This is just similar to how you would write a software library with generic interfaces that consumers will customize for their needs.

    """

class CT_Bookmark(BaseOxmlElement):
    """The ``<w:bookmarkStart>`` element"""
    name = RequiredAttribute('w:name', ST_String)
    bmrk_id = RequiredAttribute('w:id', ST_RelationshipId)

    @property
    def _next_id(self):
        """
        The first ``w:id`` unused by a ``<w:bookmarkStart>`` element, starting at
        1 and filling any gaps in numbering between existing ``<w:bookmarkStart>``
        elements.
        """
        bmrk_id_strs = self.xpath('.//w:bookmarkStart/@w:id')
        bmrk_ids = [int(bmrk_id_str) for bmrk_id_str in bmrk_id_strs]
        for num in range(1, len(bmrk_ids)+2):
            if num not in bmrk_ids:
                break
        return str(num)

    @property
    def is_closed(self):
        """
        The `is_closed` property of the :class:`CT_BookmarkRange` object is
        used to determine whether there is already a bookmarkEnd element in
        the document containing the same bookmark id. If this is the case, the
        bookmark is closed if not, the bookmark is open.
        """
        root_element = self.getroottree().getroot()
        matching_bookmarkEnds = root_element.xpath(
            './/w:bookmarkEnd[@w:id=\'%s\']' % self.bmrk_id
        )
        if not matching_bookmarkEnds:
            return False
        return True

class CT_MarkupRange(BaseOxmlElement):
    """The ``<w:bookmarkEnd>`` element."""
    bmrk_id = RequiredAttribute('w:id', ST_RelationshipId)

    @property
    def is_closed(self):
        """
        The `is_closed` property of the :class:`CT_BookmarkRange` object is
        used to determine whether there is already a bookmarkEnd element in
        the document containing the same bookmark id. If this is the case, the
        bookmark is closed if not, the bookmark is open.
        """
        root_element = self.getroottree().getroot()
        matching_bookmarkEnds = root_element.xpath(
            './/w:bookmarkEnd[@w:id=\'%s\']' % self.bmrk_id
        )
        if not matching_bookmarkEnds:
            return False
        return True

```

The mime type of the file indicates that it is a Google Docs file. These files require you to specify the file type you want to download it as. For example: Download a Google Drive Document as Microsoft Word file. 
In order to download all files from GDrive (which seems to be what you are doing) I would suggest you define a dict which maps the Google Drive file type to its desired download mime type.

In your code prior to the loop, define a dict similar to this:
```
mimetypes = {
    # Drive Document files as MS Word files.
    'application/vnd.google-apps.document': 'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document',

    # Drive Sheets files as MS Excel files.
    'application/vnd.google-apps.spreadsheet': 'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet'

    # etc.
}
```
You can find a list of the mime types Google Drive uses [here](https://developers.google.com/drive/v2/web/mime-types), and a list of mime types you can convert these to [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/4212861/what-is-a-correct-mime-type-for-docx-pptx-etc/4212908#4212908).

You can then adapt the loop to handle these mimetypes:

```
for file1 in file_list:
    download_mimetype = ''
    if file1['mimeType'] in mimetypes:
        download_mimetype = mimetypes[file1['mimeType']]

    file1.GetContentFile(file1['title'], mimetype=download_mimetype)
```
**Note**: You don't need to create a new file object to download the file. Further, the maximum file size you can download with PyDrive is currently limited by the size of your memory.

Have a look at [this PyDrive issue](https://github.com/googledrive/PyDrive/issues/64) for an example of requesting a file with a specific mimetype, and if `mimetype=mimetype` seems confusing have a look at the [Python docs on Keyword Arguments](https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/controlflow.html#keyword-arguments).

Hope this helps!

Hi Eelco,

That is odd about the vector-Laplacians.  Have you tried "super-nodes",
that is storing the matrix as a BSR matrix?  This makes sense if you have 2
degrees of freedom at the same spatial location.  That would imply a BSR
matrix with 2x2 blocks.  If you have more degrees of freedom at the same
spatial location, the blocks just grow in size.  Passing in such a BSR
matrix to SA usually helps with interpolation, and lets you use block
relaxation.

Otherwise, the vector-Laplacian results are odd.  It makes me wonder if
there isn't something to tweak regarding strength of connection or some
other parameter.  If your GMG ends up working for these problems, then
there should be some way to get SA to work as well.

- Jacob

> get really wonky eigenvectors. Solving a magnetostatics problem in terms of

nothing obvious but the fact the os.remove could fail .. rewrote the code that way .. and please do use os.rename when you generate the file .. 10 second may not be enough for the generation. Also I flush stdout after each command and wait for the initial configuration file forever without doing anything is not.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import os
from sys import stdout, stderr
from time import sleep
# from subprocess import call

def handle_content (generator,known):
    for line in generator:
        ip = line.strip()
        if ip.startswith('-'):
            if ip in known:
                del known[ip]
                stdout.write('withdraw route ' + ip[1:] + ' next-hop 192.0.2.1' + '\n')
                stdout.flush()
            # finished or trying to remove a ip we did not announce
            continue

        key = '-' + ip
        command = 'announce route ' + ip + ' next-hop 192.0.2.1 community [65532:666]' + '\n'
        known[key] = command
        stdout.write(command)
        stdout.flush()


seen = {}
filename = '/etc/exabgp/ip-blacklist.conf'

# Loop endlessly as it is what ExaBGP needs, and do nothing until a configuration is generated
while True:
    # using stderr for text as stdout is for exabgp commands only ...
    stderr.write('thomas is happy ... most of the time\n')
    stderr.flush()

    if os.path.isfile(filename):
        # Allow process creating the file to finish
        # ----------------
        # ugly .. the file should be generated with another name and then moved when completed (which is atomic).
        sleep(10)
        # ----------------
        with open(filename, "r") as line_generator:
            handle_content(line_generator,seen)

        # if we just started, we should delete previous delta if any as not relevant
        filename = '/etc/exabgp/ip-deltafile.conf'
        try:
            os.remove(filename)
        except:
            pass

    # Check every 5 seconds if an incremental update is available
    sleep (5)
```

I don't know if this should be a docker-py issue or a docker issue.  The reason for the timeout is that docker is exporting the tar file to disk before streaming the result back to the API.  This is obvious when you look at /var/lib/docker/tmp/docker-export-XXXXX when exporting a large image.  

In theory, docker should be able to stream the layers through the web server so that the client can just read the stream while the container is being exported.

Maybe the real feature wanted here is -- similar to the "payload transparency" stuff -- actually app-visible hooks for serialization? That is, the ability for a WAMP client to add its own serializer type (i.e. beyond things defined in the spec). The end-to-end encryption/"payload transparency" being just one special case of this?

Basically, it's kind of an either/or from the router's perspective: either the client (or CALL, if per-endpoint granularity) is using some format the router can decode **or** it is not. If "not" (e.g. it's encrypted to the client, or using a serializer that the router doesn't speak) then all the router can do is pass the bytes along.

If the answer is "yes", of course then the router can re-encode things for other clients (e.g. A speaks only JSON and B speaks only msgpack).

If we're going to allow "pass along raw bytes" for the end-to-end encryption feature, it doesn't seem like much more effort to also/instead allow basically the same for unknown serializations (although, you'd maybe need to pass along the name of the serializer with the bytes?). This also points to "per-endpoint" being the correct granularity (so you can have e2e encrypted endpoints alongside un-encrypted ones).

I guess my suggestion here boils down to: add an API for clients to register new serializer-types. This comes with warnings that you lose the ability for the router to re-encode. Also extend the API as suggested by @oberstet above to allow endpoints to specify a serializer. Crossbar would provide at least one such plugin out-of-the-box, which would be NaCl secretbox end-to-end encryption (but, one could imagine different encryption schemes for example, so even for this use-case exposing an API to register custom ones seems like a good idea).

It doesn't seem like just telling people to use Docker is really a good enough solution though, in our case for example, we're developing a library for other people to run on their own machines.

If your iterator is not lazy, this all happens upfront, right?  But you said GPU utilization is low, so you're probably using a lazy iterator?  One option that we've done before is create a preprocessing script that does expensive data processing upfront, and then write a simpler dataset reader that reads the preprocessed data.  This would be a lot easier than writing multi-threading code, I think.

Yeah, we've talked about having some kind of multi-processing to have the data loading and training going in separate threads when you're loading data lazily.  It's a lot of work, and it's not very high priority for us right now.  If someone wants to contribute it, we'd appreciate it, but we're not likely to get to this any time soon.

I'm closing this for now.  Feel free to re-open it if you have more questions.

Add a config variable in pytest_server_fixtures.__init__:
```
DEFAULT_SERVER_FIXTURES_RETHINK_LOOP_TYPE = None
CONFIG=FixtureConfig(

  rethink_loop_type==os.getenv('SERVER_FIXTURES_RETHINK_LOOP_TYPE', DEFAULT_SERVER_FIXTURES_RETHINK_LOOP_TYPE),
  ... 
)
```

Add a constructor argument to the RethinkDBServer class:
    
```
class RethinkDBServer(TestServer):
    random_port = True

    def __init__(self, loop_type=CONFIG.rethink_loop_type, **kwargs):
        global rethinkdb
        try:
            import rethinkdb
        except ImportError:
            pytest.skip('rethinkdb not installed, skipping test')
        super(RethinkDBServer, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        if loop_type:
           rethinkdb.set_loop_type(loop_type)
            
```

@carver @pipermerriam I pushed a new version.

This doesn't yet take the fork of our top peer into account (see my concerns in the previous comment) but other than that I think it may be a good first start. Here's how it works:

1. There's an abstract `BaseTransactionValidator` that one can implement to tell the tx pool how to handle tx validation.

2. The `TxPool` expects to get an instance of `BaseTransactionValidator` passed upon instantiation

3. There's a `DefaultTransactionValidator` that works as follows:

    A. It expects a `Chain(Proxy)` and a `BaseTransaction` that serves as initial transaction class.
    B. On validation, it will check if the chain is operating on a newer fork than what the initial transaction 
        class belongs to. If yes, then it will use that class, if not then it will use the class that was passed as 
       `initial_tx_class`

4. The class that is used as `initial_tx_class` isn't hardcoded but instead is defined in `MainnetFullNode`, `MainnetLightNode`, `RopstenFullNode` and `RopstenLightNode`. It is set to `ByzantiumTransaction` as that is the minimal criteria that we know every tx must satisfy in order to be relayed on the ethereum network today.

@moonso The key names are predefined by VEP. Unfortunately the key names have changed in recent versions (between v87 and v90). And they have also replaced ExAC with gnomAD exomes (ie not WGS). I've summarized the key names for v87 and v90 below (with links to the documentation)

VEP 87
------
**GMAF** - 1000G all popluations combined
**xxx_MAF** - 1000G (or NHLBI-ESP) individual populations
**ExAC_MAF** - ExAC all popluations
**ExAC_Adj_MAF**- ExAC all popluations (adjusted)
**ExAC_xxx_MAF** - ExAC individual populations
[http://dec2016.archive.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/vep_formats.html](http://dec2016.archive.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/vep_formats.html)

VEP 90
------
**AF** - 1000G all populations combined
**xxx_AF** - 1000G (or NHLBI-ESP) individual populations
**gnomAD_AF** - gnomAD exomes, all populations combined
**gnomAD_xxx_AF** - gnomAD exomes, individual populations
**MAX_AF** - Max of all populations (1000G, gnomAD exomes, ESP)
[https://www.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/vep_formats.html](https://www.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/vep_formats.html)

One problem is that you did not close the `bamfile`. This means that `pysam.sort` will try to sort an empty file. It works when you close the interpreter because files are then automatically closed and flushed to disk. Best way to handle this woud be to use a `with` statement: `with open(...) as bamfile: ...`.

if os.environ.get('VUNIT_SIMULATOR_DOCKER_IMG') is not None:

```

This is not a bug but the intended behavior. Each probe has independent values of threshold, timeout, etc. If you do not specify values, the default values for the command are used.

**application-gateway probe create -h**
```
Arguments
    --gateway-name      [Required]: The name of the application gateway.
    --host              [Required]: The name of the host to send the probe.
    --name -n           [Required]: The name of the probe.
    --path              [Required]: The relative path of the probe. Valid paths start from "/".
    --protocol          [Required]: The HTTP settings protocol.  Allowed values: Http, Https.
    --resource-group -g [Required]: Name of resource group.
    --interval                    : The time interval in seconds between consecutive probes.
                                    Default: 30.
    --no-wait                     : Do not wait for the long running operation to finish.
    --threshold                   : The number of failed probes after which the back end server is
                                    marked down.  Default: 8.
    --timeout                     : The probe timeout in seconds.  Default: 120.
```

@troydai Thanks for looking into it. 

For comparison, with AWS CLI, the destination file name is optional and a directory or a bucket name can be given instead :
```bash
$ aws s3 cp local-file.txt s3://bucket-name/
$ aws s3 cp s3://bucket-name/local-file.txt .
```
Meanwhile, with Azure CLI, both source and destination file names need to be given explicitly even if those are the same:
```bash
$ az storage blob upload --file local-file.txt --name local-file.txt --container-name container-name
$ az storage blob download --name local-file.txt --file local-file.txt --container-name container-name
```
Note that how much longer the commands with Azure CLI are than the same commands with AWS CLI.

Hi @MatsumotoHiroko, here are some clarifications concerning timeouts:

First, there are 2 kinds of timeout:
- socket timeout, which you have tried already. It governs how long the client(SDK) waits for the server to send data before declaring this try as failed.
- server timeout, which is passed to each method that makes a network call (for example put_block). This one governs how long the server waits for the operation to complete before responding to the client saying the request has timed out/failed.

If either timeout occurs, retries would be made by the SDK. By default, it would retry 3 times, and each time wait for a longer amount of time. Please take a look at the [sample](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/master/samples/advanced/client.py#L42) if you are interested in configuring your own retry policy. 

If all retries fail, an AzureException would be thrown like this:
`HTTPSConnectionPool(host='your_account.blob.core.windows.net', port=443): Read timed out. (read timeout=[your timeout])`

From your question, I think socket timeout is indeed the one you should use, however 1 second seems a bit too short. Please let me know if you have any other question.

Hi,

our method is intended to extract the rotational part of an arbitrary
3x3 matrix. The inertia tensor is symmetric and therefore contains no 
rotation information. Hence, the rotational part is zero.

To make this a bit clearer. Our method can be seen as a kind of polar 
decomposition which decomposes a matrix A in a orthogonal matrix R and a 
symmetric matrix S:

A = R*S.

Our method ensures that R is always a rotation matrix and contains no 
reflection. If A is already symmetric, then R will be the identity matrix.

What you search for is a main axis transformation which diagonalizes the 
inertia tensor. This can be done by an SVD. A polar decomposition is not 
the same as an SVD and cannot be directly used to diagonalize the 
tensor. But an SVD can also be used to extract the rotational part of a 
matrix.

Hope that helps,

Jan

In the example browser with one of the multithreaded demos, if you select
certain task schedulers (TBB for one) and then play with the "thread count"
slider it ends up creating and destroying many threads. And this can cause
the thread index to exceed 64 even though only a small number of threads
are being used.
I think it can be fixed by having the example browser reset the thread
indices when switching schedulers or adjusting the thread count.

What's happening here is a result of [this line](https://github.com/codemirror/CodeMirror/blob/52e48afa6d3d508c83115c142c3f8fd0dc477108/lib/codemirror.js#L4002) in CodeMirror. The logic is a bit subtle, but I will try to explain as best I can.

When you scroll using the trackpad or the mouse wheel, the browser delivers `'wheel'` events to the DOM element under the cursor. These events bubble up the DOM like most other mouse events, and if `preventDefault()` is not called on the event, the default action will be to scroll the element/page. For kinetic (momentum) scrolling, the browser continues to generate `'wheel'` events until the scrolling comes to rest. As the page is scrolled, the element under the cursor will change and hence the element receiving the `'wheel'` events will change.

This explains the immediate observation that the jitter (slow scrolling) only occurs when the cursor is over a CodeMirror cell. When the cursor is in the left margin of the notebook, or in-between CodeMirror cells, the CodeMirror `'wheel'` event handler is not invoked.

The line of code linked above prevents the event default action, which means the browser **will not** scroll the page when that code path is taken. So, why do we get *some* vertical scrolling during jitter, instead of *none*? The answer is that a wheel event can contain both `deltaX` and `deltaY` values simultaneously. When using an actual mouse wheel, the `deltaX` will be zero. But when using a trackpad, `deltaX` will only sometimes be zero (because humans are imprecise).

When CodeMirror encounters any non-zero `deltaX` value, it will take the code path linked above, which will unconditionally prevent the default scroll action. Presumably, this is done so that horizontally scrolling the editor does not *also* horizontally scroll the page. This is a reasonable behavior, but leads to the unfortunate issue we are seeing here where it falsely prevents the vertical scroll of the page. When scrolling with a trackpad, what we end up with is a stream of `'wheel'` events with small `deltaX` values, some of which are zero. As those events are processed by CodeMirror, it prevents the default scroll action on all except the ones with a zero `deltaX`. It is *these* events which actually get handled by the browser and cause the vertical scroll. But since they are only processed sporadically, the result is jitter.

There is [a clause](https://github.com/codemirror/CodeMirror/blob/52e48afa6d3d508c83115c142c3f8fd0dc477108/lib/codemirror.js#L3974) near the top of CodeMirror's `'wheel'` event handler, which bails early if there is no content to scroll. This explains why the jitter is only observed for CodeMirror cells which have overflowing content (i.e. visible scrollbars).

I can see a few solutions for this problem:
  1. Comment out the line which prevents the event default in the version of CodeMirror we ship. I tested this locally and it works fine. The downside is possible horizontal scrolling of the page when horizontally scrolling the cell contents. This probably won't actually be a problem since the notebook itself rarely has horizontal scrollbars. It could be a problem in a larger app though. This is the simplest "solution".
  2. Apply some form of thresholding to the `deltaX` value in the version of CodeMirror we ship, so that only significant `deltaX` values are actually handled by CodeMirror. This is also reasonably simple.
  3. Update the code cell so that only the active editing cell is actually a live CodeMirror instance. At all other times, the code cell should just be a static rendering of the code mirror content. [CodeMirror.runMode](https://codemirror.net/demo/runmode.html) can be used for this. This is a bit of work, but is the cleanest solution and would also likely solve some other performance concerns we have. IMHO this is the "right" way to solve the problem.

A few other random thoughts:
  - This issue probably only affects OSX users using trackpads. These pads allow simultaneous X and Y scrolling. My experience with other trackpads is that they will "lock" so that only a single axis is scrolled at a time. [Relevant Stack Exchange](http://apple.stackexchange.com/questions/106935/fix-mavericks-vertical-scrolling-lock-jerky-scrolling-accidental-safari-back).
  - CodeMirror is using deprecated APIs on `'wheel'` event (`'wheelDeltaX'` vs `'deltaX'`, etc.) which generate very different numbers from the standard APIs. This may exacerbate the situation. The standard APIs are support by all major browsers back to IE9.
  - I think it may be worth pinging Marijn to get a bit more insight into his intentions behind preventing the default scroll action, and the use of the deprecated event APIs.

Let me revise my previous statement.

Obviously, there is a difference between `obj.field = None` and `obj.field = ModelType(EmptyModel)`. The latter is an actual object even if it has nothing to export.

It's just that, under default settings, this is such an artificial scenario so as not to matter.

However, the `serialize_when_none` switch will de facto turn any instance into an `EmptyModel` if it doesn't contain at least one value. This is a common occurrence and actually pretty bizarre if you think about it.

I mean, it's fine to have an option for filtering out empty values, but that process shouldn't be able to reduce something to nothing.

(As a database analogy, a foreign key reference doesn't magically become null just because the referenced row is updated to only contains nulls.)

So I guess `{}` would in fact be the most logical representation for a `ModelType` field that contains an instance but has no values to export. This would distinguish it from a field that truly contains `None`.

Just a thought, 
```
class A(Model):
    ...
class B(A):
    ignore_fields=('x', 'y',)

# or
b = B(..., ignore_fields=['x', 'y'])
b.validate()   # auto partial
```
Many ways, all to improve DRY.

On 31 January 2017 at 03:54, Cory Johns <notifications@github.com> wrote:

Consider three handlers, a() triggers b() and b() triggers c(). Lets say
> a() runs, queuing b(). Now upgrade-charm kicks in
>
> I didn't mean to imply that the queue would not be fully depleted within a
> single Juju context, nor that each handler would be processed within a
> separate Juju context. If a() queues b(), then b() should be processed
> within the same Juju context.
>
Ok. So if ...

- the enqueued handlers are all processed within the same Juju context
- hooks have the enqueued handlers processed in their existing Juju context

... then this is the same behaviour as the current implementation as far as
hooks (and actions) are concerned. Maybe the implementation is refactored,
but existing charms should not care and keep working fine.


> I think it would be more clear to say that I am arguing for the handler
> conditions test phase to be done on demand at the time that a flag is
> changed and that—changing flags and thus adding handlers to the queue—can
> be done outside of a Juju context. If handlers are added to the queue and
> there's no existing Juju context (or, if we want to make it simpler,
> unconditionally), schedule one specifically for depleting the queue via
> juju-run.
>
I am unsure exactly what happens if you do the handler conditions tests at
the time that a flag is changed. I'd need to see some examples of what
doesn't work now that will work under the proposal, and vice versa. I
understand that it can fix things like @when_file_changed to work reliably.

Changing states/flags and having the relevant handlers queued and run seems
fairly uncomplicated to write. Apart from needing a lock to ensure that
unitdata.kv() changes don't collide with active hooks or actions, I think
it requires no modifications to the existing code.



> In my mind, the hook context is a transaction. A main thrust of this
> proposals seems to be to make handlers their own transaction (so if a
> handler successfully runs, its reactive state changes persist and will not
> be rolled back if a subsequent handler fails).
>
> I actually wasn't trying to argue for this, but I do think it would be
> better if handlers were transactional in respect to reactive flags, to
> remove inconsistencies and idiosyncrasies in when handlers will be re-run,
> but that does not mean they need to have their own Juju context. Hook
> contexts (or non-hook Juju contexts such as from actions or juju-run) are
> somewhat transactional, but they are not the only possible level of
> transactionality, and they're not actually fully transactional with respect
> to the entire state of the system.
>
> If you run handlers a(), b() in the same hook context and b() fails, then
> any changes a() made to the hook environment are rolled back.
>
> This is not true. The only changes that are currently rolled back are that
> the unitdata, include reactive states, is not committed. Any other
> changes the handler made to the system are not rolled back, because they
> are outside the scope of the framework, and Juju itself doesn't roll back
> anything that I'm aware of. I think that it makes more sense for successful
> handlers to have their flag changes committed, so that they are not
> needlessly run again (though idempotency should make it so that it doesn't
> matter).
>
I think we are good if handlers are not individually transactional, and the
all the charms.reactive state changes rolled back if one of the handlers
being executed in the hook context fails.

If a hook or action fails, Juju rolls back any changes made to the hook
context (ie. leader-set & relation-set). And if the handler is not
reinvoked (because it succeeded), any signals it sent with leader-set or
relation-set are lost.


> we also lose $JUJU_HOOK_NAME, $JUJU_RELATION_NAME, $JUJU_RELATION_ID and
> $JUJU_REMOTE_UNIT
>
> Those environment variables are still available during the hook context,
> but an important premise of reactive charms is that they shouldn't depend
> on being run in any particular hook context since it's often impossible to
> know the exact hook that will see all of the conditions for a given handler
> met.
>
Yes, I just mention these since any code using them for more than debug
output suffers from the same problems @hook has, and cannot be run in
arbitrary hook contexts. I've been thinking that my problems with
RelationBase are not because it uses `@hook`, but because it uses the
default $JUJU_RELATION_ID argument to relation-set, and calling methods
from non-@hook handlers would fail. But that is unrelated to this proposal
:)

As we move toward explicitly defining MDS and FS objects in the beamline configs, do the following on `import databroker`:

If MDS or FS raise because they can't find global config, warn, and do not import the top-level funcs (get_events, get_table, DataBroker). It's *very* unusual to have contingent imports but I think alternatives (require global config forever; replace `DataBroker` with `None`...) are worse.

The whole issue is that `DataBroker`, `get_events`, and friends are importable from the top level `__init__` and that import is *very* ingrained with staff at this point. For this reason, `from databroker import db` should continue to work forever for users that have a global config in place.

One solution is adding a version specifier to the `Field` class:

```python
class Employee(Entity):
    name = StringField()
    title = StringField(version=('foreman', '>=', '1.7.0'))
```

I don't like this solution very much, though. I see at least one big problem. The version specifier above is only capable of expressing the statement "this field exists in foreman version 1.7.0 and above." There may be other versioning differences, though. For example, what if you wanted to express the statement "this field has a minimum length of three characters in foreman version 1.7.0 and above." You cannot express that information with the above design.

.Net Core (netcoreapp1.0) is a platform as is .NET Framework (net451). Platforms have various behaviors that may not be consistent across eachother. .NET Standard is a view over multiple platforms where you can say that you want to write code against what they have in common. So if both of those platforms support .NET Standard 1.6, then you can just write a library that targets netstandard1.6 and load it in both and have high confidence it will "work everywhere".

In short, if you are building general purpose libraries, use netstandardX.X. If you are writing libraries that will only work on .NET Core because you need to depend on behaviors that are only there, use netcoreappX.X. 

In the end, we hope that only applications, not libraries need to use the platforms and all but a few special case libraries can be .NET Standard.

Hope that helps.

Using GzipFIle.tell and .seek didn't work because they use positions in decompressed stream, GzipFile.fileobj.seek/tell didn't work because files are buffered, so if data is read it doesn't mean it is processed; re-opening these files with buffering turned off didn't work as well, because GzipFile adds another buffer. 

I haven't found a way to get an exact position with GzipFile - it seems this could require copy-pasting a lot of code. Also, I noticed that for concatenated gzip files GzipFIle may not necessarily detect a compression failure, and start producing broken json lines instead, so detecting a point where GzipFile fails doesn't necessarily gives a right position to restart decoding from.

The issue here is that `rrulebase` caches the length after the first call to `count()`, and this cache is not invalidated when changes are made to the rruleset:

```python
from datetime import datetime as dt
from dateutil import rrule as rr

dts = dt(2015, 8, 11)
dte = dt(2016, 8, 11)

If it helps, we can speed up the leap year calculation by a factor of 10 by writing our own code that avoids the `calendar.isleap` call within a list comprehension. 

`
import time
import calendar
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from datetime import datetime

from timeit import timeit

def leap(year):
    leapyear = (np.mod(year, 4) == 0) & ((np.mod(year, 100) != 0) | (np.mod(year, 400) == 0))
    return leapyear
    
idx = pd.DatetimeIndex(start=datetime(1999, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0), end=datetime(2005, 12, 31, 0, 0, 0), freq='1H')

isleap = [calendar.isleap(t.year) for t in idx]

print(timeit(stmt="isleap = [calendar.isleap(t.year) for t in idx]", 
                             number=1000, 
                             globals={'idx': idx, 'calendar': calendar}))

yrs = np.asarray(idx.year)
alt_isleap = leap(yrs)

print(timeit(stmt="alt_isleap = leap(np.asarray(idx.year))", 
                             number=1000, 
                             globals={'idx': idx, 'np': np, 'leap': leap}))
`

I've looked through the changes to pysystem and singlediode_methods.  I'll echo Will's thanks for all the substantial work done here - thanks!  I'm looking forward to seeing this merged when we all concur. 

I agree with Will's general comments above.  I also will need some time to go through this in detail. Concerns from my scan of the code:
- pvsystem.py - most of my concerns relate to documentation and inline comments.  It's an opportunity for a thorough edit for consistency and clarity, there's nothing wrong, just as I'm reading it I'm thinking that we can be more clear. Perhaps this is something I can do, but I don't know how to pull a copy of the file, edit and push changes.
- I am not a fan of the kwarg values 'slow' and 'fast', nor of their use in function names.  We should use terms analogous to lambertw and bishop88, perhaps brentq and newton instead. I'm not a fan of function names that don't indicate what they do, 'bishop88' is an example.  I'd prefer a verb-led name like 'solve_IV_curve_bishop88' or similar, with the method added as a suffix.
- pvsystem.singlediode contains an explicit sequence of steps if method=lambertw, but only a call to singlediode_methods.fxxx if method is otherwise.  This looks like a maintenance problem in the future.  I'd prefer to package the lambertw material into a singlediode_methods function and call it using the 'sdm_fun' approach being used for the 'faster' and 'slower' ways.
- we should write functions that return the expansion of singleton parameters, and the calculation of voltages (when ivcurve_pnts is supplied) up to singlediode, instead of dealing with these issues within each method.  Each method function should expect to receive the parameters already expanded and the requested voltages for each curve (keep in mind that, with only ivcurve_pnts as the argument) the voltage strings will be different for each curve.
- the v_from_i, I_from_v, and mpp methods in pvsystem should follow the same convention as singlediode(), i.e., using a 'method' kwarg to direct the calculation to a specific method.
- for 'newton' aka 'fast' I'm concerned about the implication in the code comments that you shouldn't trust the method.  Can we do something here to help the user decide if they are at risk of a bad calculation?  Maybe this would be an enhancement best left for future work.

With this change, we should also define exactly what we consider to be a stream (or a valid input to a `SimpleDataStream`). Do we consider iterable objects (has an `__iter__`method which returns an iterator; or defines `__getitem__`) or iterator objects (has a `next` (Python 2) or `__next__ `(Python 3) method.) as valid input? The main difference is that an if we call `iter(iterable)` multiple times, we will get independent iterators, whereas we can only iterate once over a single iterator.

In the context of GluonNLP DataStream this means, do we want to allow iterating over a DataStream multiple times? Or do we consider a DataStream to be a throwaway object that should only be used once? (It is cheap to create a new DataStream).

This PR proposed using generator expression `transformed = (transform(s) for s in stream)` instead of of `stream.transform()`. Note that the generator expression always returns a iterator. `stream.transform()` however always returns a iterable, meaning that if the underlying stream is an iterable it is possible to iterate multiple times over the transformed stream (and in case the underlying stream is an iterator, it is only possible to iterate once over the transformed stream).

Consequently, using generator expressions only makes sense if we only require the stream to be an iterator. If we decide to require the Stream to be an iterable I will revert the respective change and add back the `.transform()` interface.

(Independently of this question, in the previous implementation `PrefetchingStream(stream).transform()` wouldn't work.)

A small example to illustrate the difference:

```
In [1]: import gluonnlp as nlp
In [2]: iterator = [1,2,3]
In [3]: iterable = (i for i in range(3))
In [4]: stream1 = nlp.data.SimpleDataStream(iterator)
In [5]: stream2 = nlp.data.SimpleDataStream(iterable)
In [6]: list(stream1)
Out[6]: [1, 2, 3]
In [7]: list(stream1)
Out[7]: [1, 2, 3]
In [8]: list(stream2)
Out[8]: [0, 1, 2]
In [9]: list(stream2)
Out[9]: []
```

So this is a bit of a mess, but I think I'm nearing the bottom of the rabbit hole :-).

The spec is basically wrong for DOP because it is a unitless property. The implementation of what is returned by this message is different on PX4 and ArduPilot:

* PX4 - Abuses the value - it returns hacc and vacc (instead of hdop and vdop, respectively). These values arrive in mm and are converted to metres (in scaled integers). 
* Ardupilot - returns  the uBlox hDOP for uBlox devices that support the MSG_DOP uBlox message (and similar for vDOP). So this is the unscaled unitless value as you would expect.

I am still chatting to the guys to see if we can "rationalise" this ... after which I'll look at the dronekit docs.

Well the same page is discussing using `GaudiExec` just a bit further down so I am not sure about the difference. I would prefer that the users are reusing the same checked out area each time rather than performing a new check-out each time. You suggestion also has several problems.

- Ganga should not automatically delete items which are created by a user action. It gives an unpredictable change of ownership.
- Resubmission will be broken if directory deleted straight afterwards. If just left in `/tmp` it might be deleted by the machine you run on and then give unpredictable results.
- If running on `lxplus` the `/tmp` area is not shared and an auto-resubmit running from a different machine will fail.

since `j1` and `j2` in this example would have different `id` values we would expect this to fail however be careful about the _exact_ comparison that's going on `==` is not the same as `is`. There should be no need for two scanners, but is there a real reason not to just create `tplink` component to homeassistant, assuming that's the use-case you are going for?

By doing so there would be no need to have almost a copy from the pyhs100's `discover.py` and/or `protocol.py`. Besides avoiding potential problems of out-of-sync copies in case the protocol changes, that would also avoid the licensing issue (netdisco being Apache 2.0 whereas pyhs100 is GPLv3+).

The ENUM binding from C++ to Python is actually done in the file you mentioned python.cpp, please refer to the line 37 #define BIND_ENUM which changes the option string get back from C++ function rs2_option_to_string to lower case and replace the space to "_" then map the string to the enum value.
For example, the RS2_OPTION_LASER_POWER return from rs2_option_to_string will be "Laser Power", and then the function will change the "Laser Power" to "laser_power" and link it to the enum value.

So as far as I can understand:

1. False -> no one will ever use this (?)
2. True -> parse basic band structure to get things like gap but don't store the full band structure in GridFS or do projected band structures (e.g., used in structure optimization and static runs)
3. "uniform" -> same as True, but also will store full band structure in GridFS and projected band structure (e.g., used in high k-mesh uniform grid runs)
4. "line" -> same as uniform, but will produce a BSSymmLine object instead (e.g., used in symm line

Isn't there any simpler way to delegate resolving to children?

I think it could be useful to add `default_value` back into `Field`, however I think it only should be applied when using the default resolver, as if not it could have side-effects if the custom resolver function returns `None`.

For that, we can do two things:
* Add `defaut_value` into Field, and modifying the [Typemap default resolver](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/master/graphene/types/typemap.py#L189) to use the default value if the attr is not found in root. Similar to `getattr(root, attname, default_value)`
* Create a resolver decorator that mimics the logic that you did.

If the latter is the best approach to make, maybe keeping the decorator in your app scope is a good case until more people have the same issue.
Let me know your thoughts!

I'm working on something with no security implications (all data is public), so s a make-shift solution (don't use in production), I'm registering (pseudo code) all models using the following approach crufty:

    import Base # = some declarative_base(cls=mixin), see [1]
    import graphene
    class Query(graphene.ObjectType): pass
    for k, v in Base._decl_class_registry.items():
        setattr(Query, k.lower(), v)
        setattr(Query, 'resolve_%s' % k, lambda self, args, info: v.get(args.get('id')).dict() #dict() coming from [1]

[1] see https://github.com/thegroovebox/api.groovebox.org/blob/master/groovebox/api/core.py#L33

Note: _decl_class_registry has some non-models registered with it (e.g. _sa_module_registry), so additional error handling / logic is required.

> 1. On the base32 spec: until a spec errata is processed, we should just roll forward with what we think is most sensible. How about we allow a lengths for the Joining Device Credential between 6 and 32, inclusive. If we do not end up with a multiple of 8 bits, simply pad the end with zeros.

Yes, the JDC length should be 6 to 32 characters as per Thread 1.1.1 spec. I was more concerned with duplicate or vague usage of the defined constants. Will take a better to look at each of them.
As for the format itself, it has to come in multiple of 40bits and it cannot simply be padded with zero-data as the padding is used to encode the length of the decoded data. Currently I see two possible solutions:
1) Use the current implementation, i.e. enforced 40bit alignment and '=' as a padding symbol.
2) Use implicit padding, i.e. treat the encoded string as if it was aligned to 40bit block with padding, but do not enforce this requirement (in decoding) or add any padding (in encoding). I guess that's what you meant, just in different words.

> 2. Note that the Joiner Device Credential and Commissioner Credential are somewhat different.
The Commissioner Credential is a utf-8 passphrase with length 6 to 255, inclusive. The Commissioner Credential is used to derive the PSKc using a key-stretching algorithm and its length is fixed at 16 bytes.

I'll leave the PSKc untouched in this PR.

> 3. Given that the Thread Certification Test Harness passes the PSKd, I think we should try to maintain a path that allows the Thread Certification Test Harness to work unmodified (you may have noticed that the Travis CI checks are failing currently because of this). This means we need to provide the ability to input both the Joiner Device Credential and the PSKd.

I'm not very familiar with the harness scripts, so correct me if I'm wrong. From a quick glance I see that it uses two interfaces node_api.py (for windows?) and node_cli.py (for linux cli?). The former uses calls to otNodeApi C++ functions, so it's simple (and logical) to pass the binary PSKd. The CLI api, however uses shell commands, which are not particularly friendly to random binary data. Current (and previous) code works only because the binary PSKd has all printable symbols, so I would assume that it's only logical that the CLI should use some form of ASCII encoding. This encoding could be provided in the interface scripts (i.e. changes only in node_cli.py functions `commissioner_add_joiner` and `joiner_start`), so the actual certification scripts would be unchanged.

> 5. Regarding the Windows project, I think it's fine to keep the API passing the PSKd and PSKc in binary form. We can add the base32 format at a later time.

otNodeApi project can be left with binary only PSKd support as mentioned in the previous paragraph. This should fix the appveyor build error. However I assume that changes in the libopenthread[_k] project should not be reverted.

Some details/reasoning from earlier conversation with `Mschwen`.

```
ktbyers [10:27 AM] 
If it was XML, wouldn't you want to get it via NX-API?
```

```
mschwen [10:27 AM] 
No, and there are multiple reasons. 
1 - not all NXOS code supports  NX-API, but all support XML output
2 - while XML Schemas change between versions, output is standard
 across platforms and versions of NXOS for XML
3 - Converting XML output to JSON Open Config model is much easier 
than trying to compensate for all of Cisco’s varying ASCII outputs
```

ktbyers [10:29 AM] 
...didn't pre-7 NX-OS not have XML?

mschwen [10:29] 
I am running NXOS 6 versions that support XML

Actually the data for the codecs - utf8Codec and binaryCodec is not a Python Dictionary, it's actual data which is going to be transferred as part of payload after encoding. In order to support the http content-type(s) - text/plain and xml, we are using utf8Codec to encode data to charset utf-8 similarly for content-type - application/octetstream, we are using base64 encoding before putting the data into payload. So, with these codecs, we are doing encoding and decoding of the message data.

@thorink I think that keeping the multiple version selection as local as possible would be the best thing. Users already rely on the global variables, so I'd be hesitant to mess with those too much (not to mention breaking the `pybind11::*` targets). A concrete proposal would be something like this:

```cmake
function(pybind11_add_module target_name)
    # ... existing `cmake_parse_arguments` code is here
    
    # Only new lines in this function
    if(ARG_PYTHON_VERSION)
         # Replace PYTHON_INCLUDE_DIRS, PYTHON_LIBRARIES, etc. in the local scope
         python_version_override(${ARG_PYTHON_VERSION})
    endif()

    # ... continue existing code (with possibly overriden locals)
endfunction()
```
where:
```cmake
function(python_version_override version)
    # ... look up data specific to ${version}
    #     cache as needed, but avoid overriding existing globals

    # set variables in parent scope only
    set(PYTHON_INCLUDE_DIRS <value> PARENT_SCOPE)
    set(PYTHON_LIBRARIES <value> PARENT_SCOPE)
    # etc.
endfunction()
```
Looking at your branch, I believe you already have the code that does all this, my suggestion is to just encapsulate everything in a single function (`python_version_override`) and only set the variables in the parent scope (local non-cache variables have priority over global cache variables in CMake). What do you think about this approach?

@dean0x7d Thanks a lot for your code snippet! With slight adjustments, it works like a charm. A few notes regarding the usage that may be helpful for other folks trying to migrate their code:

* For the Python -> C++ direction, a member variable `value` is used. It is implicitly created when using the `PYBIND11_TYPE_CASTER` macro. It assumes `Type` to be default-constructible. `boost::variant` is not always default constructible, but it is when its first component type is. I had to add a default constructor to fix this.
* The converter needs to be available in the _compilation unit_ where it would be needed. It is not sufficient to declare it in a separate C++ file, because the function that requires the conversion has no knowledge of this. This is in contrast to `boost::python` where there is a static converter registry. Not a big deal, but something to watch out for.

Thanks again!

ok we don't really want to call start before reload. but we only need the stdout and stderr if it failed to start. so! there's not really a problem putting the `nginx` call inside the "if we fail to reload", I think.

so let's see:
1. problem with the config: fail to reload, which gives us a bad returncode. fail to start, which returns with an error we ignore. we test the returncode, which says it failed, so we try to start again, and this time when it fails we print the error. (it terminates on failure to start.) all good!
2. was running before: reload successfully. all good!
3. wasn't running before but could be: fail to reload, which gives us a bad returncode. start successfully, which returns without an error, and we don't catch the output but that's fine because it went well. we test the returncode, which says everything is fine, so we move on. all good!

so, we only try to catch the output if we know we've failed to start. which is rare enough that it's fine to call it twice there.

> For example, a distro packager will want to use the system lz4 library irrespective of whether the bundled one is newer.

The line would still be

```
      sos execute {task} -v {verbosity} -s {sig_mode} \
        {'--dryrun' if run_mode == 'dryrun' else ''}
```
but we have a problem here.

We now submit jobs in batches to task engine. Say if there are 4 `max_running_jobs` and 100 tasks, we will submit 25 tasks to each `process`. Current implementation does

```
sos execute task1 task2 ...
```
Now, with the `job_template` stuff, are we going to do

```
sos execute {' '.join(tasks)} ...
```
or multiple
```
sos execute {task}
```
The patch I just submitted uses the latter but stacks all jobs to a single file, something like
```
$ cat adaef96f81afbf48-5fc993c7cb44264f.sh
sos execute adaef96f81afbf48

sos execute 8641a250adbb84fd

sos execute e2e6870014f2ed04

sos execute 5fc993c7cb44264f
```
but this might not work if your `job_template` has something complicated.

@jph00 : I've just tested the change.

**tl;dr:** PR works well, both OK at runtime, PR more semantically correct.

---

**Details:**

Here are the original initialization lines:

```python
self.out = nn.Linear(em_sz_dec*2, len(itos_dec))
self.out.weight.data = self.emb_dec.weight.data
```

Because the weights are replaced right away, the init value actually doesn't matter.

In the end, `self.out.weight.data` will have the same shape as if it had been initialized with `em_sz_dec` directly.

Both versions (original and this PR) work the same at run time, but `em_sz_dec` is the one corresponding to the actual shape of the linear layer.

**More verbose output:**

Code:

```python

self.out.weight.data = self.emb_dec.weight.data

```

Hey,

a good way to send messages to tasks in the current API is to use actors. Each training task is a long running actor which has a train() method that does the training for a few steps (which takes say a few seconds); you can then send messages to the actor by invoking methods on it, for example for killing it or for changing some training settings, etc. These can be invoked in between the training tasks.

-- Philipp.

Cool. [tvcatchup.py](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/master/src/streamlink/plugins/tvcatchup.py) is a pretty basic plugin.

To make a `Plugin` you have to subclass `Plugin` and set the module level variable `__plugin__` to that class (`__plugin__ = TVCatchup`). Then you have to implement the two required methods, `can_handle_url` (a classmethod) and `_get_streams`. 

`can_handle_url` is super simple, you just return `True`/`False` if this plugin can handle the supplied URL. Normally this is done wit a regex, (`r"http://(?:www\.)?tvcatchup.com/watch/\w+"`). 

`_get_streams` is also pretty simple, you have either return a `list`, `iterable`, or `dict` containing the streams. The `dict` must have the quality name (eg. `540p`) as the key and an instance of `Stream` as the value, likewise with the `list`/`iterable` it must be a `tuple` of `(quality, stream)`. There are a number of different `Stream` classes in the [stream module](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/tree/master/src/streamlink/stream). The most common one now is probably `HLSStream` (as in tvcatchup). 
There is a special global `http` which is a [`requests.Session`](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/) instance (with some extra methods for json/xml), which has been set up with the users preferences, you should use this to do all http requests. You can see in the `tvcatchup` plugin, the page (`self.url`) is requested and an `m3u8` URL is searched for, if it is found then it is parsed with `HLSStream.parse_variant_playlist` if the URL contains `_adp`. `HLSStream.parse_variant_playlist` returns a properly formatted `dict` with all the streams. In the other case (`_adp` not in the URL) it assumes it's `576p` and returns a `dict` with one `HLSStream` instance.

If you fire up a python shell and do
```python
import streamlink
streamlink.streams("http://tvcatchup.com/watch/bbcone")
```
you will see what the plugin returns :) In this case the quality keys are in bitrate (`550k`, etc). Streamlink sorts the qualities returned by the `Plugin` (see [`stream_weight`](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/master/src/streamlink/plugin/plugin.py#L35) to see how the weights are compared). 

To write your own plugin you need to work out how the site works, and how it gets it stream urls (m3u8/f4m/etc.). Then it's just a case of getting your `Plugin` to replicate it :)
